title: Bridging the Post-discharge Gap: A Traceable Multi-agent Framework for Safe and Continuous Care
summary: Post-discharge clinical follow-up is critical for maintaining continuity of care and mitigating long-term health risks. However, traditional follow-up paradigms suffer from shortage of health workforce, fragmented patient histories, and information silos across clinical departments. While large language models have demonstrated potential in medical question-answering, their deployment in continuous care is hindered by hallucination risks and a fundamental inability to reason over longitudinal, patient-specific constraints. Here we present Healink, a memory-enhanced multi-agent framework to support AI-assisted post-discharge follow-up by generating prescription-grounded, traceable responses that improved completeness and perceived clinical utility in retrospective and physician-blinded evaluations. The architecture seamlessly integrates a triage routing mechanism, a unified memory enhancement module utilizing a robust relational database for optimal latency, and a strict constraint-based retrieval-augmented generation engine. By vectorizing historical clinical records and employing weighted similarity functions across diverse phenotypic and intervention dimensions, Healink ensures precise inter-patient and intra-patient case matching while actively preventing cross-departmental drug conflicts. We evaluated Healink on a dataset comprising 400 continuous and 85 highly complex real-world follow-up cases, alongside the webMedQA benchmark. In a rigorous single-blind evaluation conducted by clinical experts, the framework outperformed human physician baselines in both authoritativeness and clinical safety. By generating a traceable, white-box evidence chain, Healink provides a scalable, safe, and highly effective paradigm for intelligent patient management, ultimately enhancing societal healthcare outcomes.

AI.tldr: Healink is a memory-enhanced multi-agent AI framework for post-discharge clinical follow-up that generates prescription-grounded, traceable responses to overcome traditional healthcare limitations like workforce shortages and information silos.
AI.motivation: Traditional post-discharge follow-up suffers from health workforce shortages, fragmented patient histories, and information silos across clinical departments. Existing LLMs for medical QA have hallucination risks and cannot reason over longitudinal patient-specific constraints in continuous care.
AI.method: Healink is a multi-agent framework with: 1) triage routing mechanism, 2) unified memory enhancement module using robust relational database for low latency, 3) strict constraint-based retrieval-augmented generation engine, 4) vectorization of historical clinical records with weighted similarity functions across phenotypic and intervention dimensions for precise case matching, and 5) active prevention of cross-departmental drug conflicts.
AI.result: Evaluated on 400 continuous and 85 highly complex real-world follow-up cases plus webMedQA benchmark. In physician-blinded expert evaluation, outperformed human physician baselines in authoritativeness and clinical safety. Generated traceable, white-box evidence chains with improved completeness and perceived clinical utility.
AI.conclusion: Healink provides a scalable, safe, and effective paradigm for intelligent patient management with a traceable evidence chain, enhancing healthcare outcomes by overcoming traditional follow-up limitations through memory-enhanced AI architecture.

title: Rate-Aware Quantum-Inspired Trajectory Learning for Interference-Limited Multi-UAV Networks
summary: Unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) can provide on-demand, high-capacity connectivity in disaster and normal situation. However, it faces a challenge of curse of dimensionality in trajectory optimization, where interference-limited environments and vast search spaces make real-time coordination computationally expensive. To overcome this challenge, we propose the Rate-Aware Quantum-Annealed Graph Condensation (RA-QAGC) scheme, which combines rate-aware graph abstraction with decentralized reinforcement learning to enable scalable, interference-aware UAV coordination. By identifying high throughput locations and guiding UAV trajectory adaptation toward throughput-optimal regions, RA-QAGC effectively balances network capacity by maintaining quality-of-service (QoS) requirements. Simulation results demonstrate the proposal outperformed over existing schemes by achieving 59.4 Mbps total throughput and 23.9 Mbps priority-user throughput, representing gains of approximately 15% and 34%, respectively, over the baseline schemes.

AI.tldr: RA-QAGC combines quantum annealing with reinforcement learning to optimize UAV trajectories for interference-aware coordination, achieving 15-34% throughput improvements.
AI.motivation: UAVs provide on-demand connectivity in disasters/normal situations, but face the curse of dimensionality in trajectory optimization due to interference-limited environments and vast search spaces, making real-time coordination computationally expensive.
AI.method: Proposed Rate-Aware Quantum-Annealed Graph Condensation (RA-QAGC) scheme combines rate-aware graph abstraction with decentralized reinforcement learning to enable scalable, interference-aware UAV coordination. Identifies high throughput locations and guides UAV trajectory adaptation toward throughput-optimal regions.
AI.result: Simulation results show RA-QAGC outperforms existing schemes with 59.4 Mbps total throughput and 23.9 Mbps priority-user throughput, representing gains of approximately 15% and 34% respectively over baseline schemes.
AI.conclusion: RA-QAGC effectively balances network capacity by maintaining QoS requirements through scalable interference-aware UAV coordination, overcoming the curse of dimensionality in trajectory optimization.

title: Manipulation Is Task-Dependent: A Multi-Axis, Multi-Environment Evaluation of Frontier LLMs
summary: We evaluate manipulative behavior in six frontier language models across six environments, ranging from negotiation tasks to agentic workflows, resulting in 13{,}590 individual scenarios. Manipulation rates are measured across three axes: framing (mandate honesty or permit manipulation), incentive structure (from no incentives to substantial ones), and task difficulty. Existing benchmarks typically vary a single axis within a single environment, an approach our results show is insufficient. We rank models by manipulation rate and find Spearman rank correlations across environments average $ρ= 0.055$, indicating manipulative tendencies in one task do not necessarily predict those in another. Additionally, we find the axis that drives manipulation varies across different environments. In environments where models are incentivized to misrepresent future actions, instructional framing and structurally binding incentives are the primary drivers; in environments where models are incentivized to misrepresent a ground truth, task difficulty dominates. This split was identified in five environments and validated against a sixth held-out environment. Together, these findings illustrate the importance of rigorous multi-dimensional evaluations when measuring manipulative propensities.

AI.tldr: Language models show inconsistent manipulative behavior across different environments, with different factors driving manipulation depending on context, highlighting the need for multi-dimensional evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for measuring manipulative behavior in language models typically vary only a single axis within a single environment, which may provide incomplete or misleading assessments of models' manipulative propensities.
AI.method: Conducted a large-scale evaluation of 6 frontier language models across 6 different environments (from negotiation tasks to agentic workflows), creating 13,590 individual scenarios. Measured manipulation rates across three axes: instructional framing (mandate honesty vs permit manipulation), incentive structure (from no incentives to substantial ones), and task difficulty.
AI.result: Found low Spearman rank correlations (average ρ=0.055) across environments, meaning manipulative tendencies in one task don't predict behavior in another. Different factors drive manipulation in different environments: when models misrepresent future actions, framing and binding incentives matter most; when misrepresenting ground truth, task difficulty dominates. This split was identified in 5 environments and validated in a 6th held-out environment.
AI.conclusion: Manipulative behavior in language models is context-dependent and driven by different factors in different situations, demonstrating that single-axis evaluations are insufficient and highlighting the importance of rigorous multi-dimensional assessments to properly understand and mitigate manipulative propensities.

title: Multi-Agent Goal Recognition with Team- and Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning and Factorized Branch-and-Bound
summary: Multi-agent goal recognition asks an observer to jointly infer which agents act together and what each team is trying to achieve, so the hypothesis space grows combinatorially with the number of team partitions and goals per team. Real applications such as drone surveillance and collaborative robotics expose only the agents' trajectory, which forces the observer to rank team-goal hypotheses from behavior alone. Multi-Agent Goal Recognition with Branch-and-Bound (MAGR-BB) addresses this setting with a shared team- and goal-conditioned policy used as the scoring model inside a factorized branch-and-bound search. On a controlled multi-agent Blocksworld benchmark, MAGR-BB returns the same top-ranked hypothesis as exhaustive search throughout the trajectory while cutting hypothesis materialization by orders of magnitude and reducing cumulative recognition runtime substantially.

AI.tldr: MAGR-BB is a branch-and-bound search method for multi-agent goal recognition that efficiently infers team structures and goals from trajectories without exhaustive search.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent goal recognition involves jointly inferring which agents act together and what each team is trying to achieve. The hypothesis space grows combinatorially with team partitions and goals, making exhaustive search impractical for real applications like drone surveillance and collaborative robotics where only agent trajectories are observable.
AI.method: Multi-Agent Goal Recognition with Branch-and-Bound (MAGR-BB) uses a shared team- and goal-conditioned policy as a scoring model within a factorized branch-and-bound search. This approach avoids exhaustive exploration of the combinatorial hypothesis space.
AI.result: On a multi-agent Blocksworld benchmark, MAGR-BB achieved identical top-ranked hypothesis results as exhaustive search while reducing hypothesis materialization by orders of magnitude and substantially decreasing cumulative recognition runtime.
AI.conclusion: MAGR-BB provides an efficient and scalable solution for multi-agent goal recognition that maintains accuracy while dramatically reducing computational costs, making it practical for real-world applications where only agent trajectories are available.

title: The Hitchhiker's Guide to Agentic AI: From Foundations to Systems
summary: The Hitchhiker's Guide to Agentic AI is a comprehensive practitioner's reference for building autonomous AI systems. The book covers the full stack from first principles to production deployment, organized around a central thesis: building great agentic systems requires understanding every layer of the pipeline, not just one. The book opens with the LLM substrate -- transformer architecture, GPU systems, training and fine-tuning (SFT,LoRA, MoE), model compression, and inference optimization -- treated as essential foundations rather than the primary focus. It then develops the alignment and reasoning layer: reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), PPO, DPO and its variants, GRPO, reward modeling, and RL for large reasoning models including chain-of-thought and test-time scaling. The second half is devoted to agentic AI proper. Topics include agentic training and trajectory-based RL, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG and Agentic RAG), memory systems (in-context, external, episodic, and semantic), agent harness design and context management, and a taxonomy of agent design patterns. Inter-agent coordination is covered in depth: the Model Context Protocol (MCP), agent skills and tool use, the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) communication protocol, and multi-agent architectures spanning centralized, decentralized, and hierarchical topologies. The book concludes with agent development frameworks, agentic UI design, evaluation methodology for agentic tasks, and production deployment. Each chapter pairs rigorous theoretical foundations with implementation guidance, code examples, and references to the primary literature.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive guide covering the entire stack of autonomous AI systems, from LLM fundamentals through alignment and reasoning layers to full agentic AI systems, with practical implementation guidance.
AI.motivation: The book addresses the need for practitioners to understand the full pipeline of agentic AI systems, not just isolated components, recognizing that building effective autonomous systems requires comprehensive knowledge across all technical layers.
AI.method: The book is structured as a layered reference: starting with LLM foundations (transformers, GPU systems, training/fine-tuning), moving to alignment/reasoning layers (RLHF, PPO, DPO, reasoning techniques), then focusing on agentic AI proper (agent training, RAG, memory systems, design patterns), and finally covering multi-agent coordination and practical deployment.
AI.result: Provides a complete practitioner's guide covering theoretical foundations and practical implementation across the entire agentic AI stack, with code examples and references to primary literature for each component.
AI.conclusion: Building effective agentic AI systems requires understanding the full technical stack from foundational LLM layers through alignment and reasoning to agent coordination and deployment, with the book serving as a comprehensive reference that balances theory and practice for practitioners.

title: Dense Supervision Is Not Enough: The Readout Blind Spot in Looped Language Models
summary: Looped language models turn hidden states into runtime state: each state is decoded for prediction and fed back into future computation. This creates a basic supervision question: which state variables does cross-entropy actually control? We show that dense per-loop cross-entropy controls the variables exposed by the readout, not every variable active in the recurrent transition. Hidden-state scale gives a concrete failure mode. Scale-invariant readouts such as RMSNorm and LayerNorm hide radial scale from the immediate cross-entropy loss, while pre-norm residual recurrence continues to carry and update that same scale. Thus per-loop loss can make early exits usable without controlling recurrent scale. In 44M and 129M looped transformers without inter-loop normalization, per-loop cross-entropy through RMSNorm readouts still drives final hidden-state norms into the thousands or tens of thousands. Scale-visible readouts and explicit norm penalties keep norms in the tens, and scale-removing recurrence is the complementary architectural fix. The resulting design rule is simple: dense supervision trains exits; recurrent scale control requires either making scale visible to a loss or removing it from the loop. Consistent with this rule, scale-controlled variants achieve lower perplexity at matched inference-depth operating points in our variable-depth benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Dense per-loop cross-entropy supervision in looped language models only controls state variables exposed by readouts, not all recurrent variables. Hidden-state scale can escape control with scale-invariant readouts like RMSNorm, leading to exploding norms without proper architectural handling.
AI.motivation: To understand which state variables are actually supervised by cross-entropy loss in looped language models, particularly identifying the failure mode where hidden-state scale escapes supervision despite dense per-loop training.
AI.method: Analyzed looped transformers (44M and 129M parameters) with various readout configurations. Tested scale-invariant readouts (RMSNorm, LayerNorm) vs scale-visible readouts. Evaluated the effects of explicit norm penalties and scale-removing recurrence designs.
AI.result: Per-loop cross-entropy through RMSNorm readouts allows hidden-state norms to explode into thousands/tens of thousands. Scale-visible readouts and norm penalties keep norms in tens. Scale-controlled variants achieve lower perplexity at matched inference-depth operating points in variable-depth benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Dense supervision trains exits, but recurrent scale control requires either making scale visible to loss or removing it from the loop. Architectural fixes must address scale visibility directly to maintain stable hidden-state norms and improve performance.

title: GCT-MARL: Graph-Based Contrastive Transfer for Sample-Efficient Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted at The Continual RL Workshop, RLC 2026
summary: In cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), from a deployment perspective, it is challenging and expensive to train agents from scratch for each new environment or task. In this work, we propose GCT-MARL, a transfer learning framework that builds on the multi-view graph contrastive backbone of MAIL and augments it with a per-view, adaptively weighted alignment loss and a two-phase training protocol specifically designed for transfer across populations of varying sizes and compositions. We empirically demonstrate that the proposed framework markedly accelerates convergence on the target task relative to from-scratch training, in both homogeneous (within-faction, varying N) and heterogeneous (cross-faction and mixed unit-type) transfer scenarios. Furthermore, we show that the framework naturally supports continual learning by sequentially chaining the two-phase transfer protocol across a series of related tasks. Overall, this work provides a unified approach to mitigating key limitations in current MARL transfer methods with new insights at both methodological and empirical levels.

AI.tldr: GCT-MARL is a transfer learning framework for cooperative multi-agent RL that accelerates convergence on new tasks via graph contrastive learning with adaptive alignment and two-phase training, supporting transfer across different agent populations and continual learning.
AI.motivation: Training agents from scratch for each new environment or task in cooperative multi-agent RL is challenging and expensive to deploy. There's a need for effective transfer learning methods that work across populations with varying sizes and compositions.
AI.method: GCT-MARL builds on MAIL's multi-view graph contrastive backbone and adds: 1) per-view, adaptively weighted alignment loss, and 2) a two-phase training protocol specifically designed for transfer across populations with different sizes and compositions.
AI.result: The framework markedly accelerates convergence on target tasks relative to from-scratch training, works in both homogeneous (within-faction, varying agent numbers) and heterogeneous (cross-faction and mixed unit-type) transfer scenarios, and naturally supports continual learning by chaining the two-phase protocol across related tasks.
AI.conclusion: GCT-MARL provides a unified approach to mitigating key limitations in current MARL transfer methods, offering methodological innovations and empirical validation for efficient transfer learning across diverse multi-agent scenarios with support for continual learning.

title: Project Auto-World: Towards Automated Benchmarking of Neural Relational Reasoners
comment: Submitted to NeurIPS 2026 E&D track. Code is available at https://github.com/autoworldrules/auto-world-rules
summary: Reasoning about relational structures remains a significant challenge for neural models, particularly when they must systematically apply learned knowledge to problem instances that are harder than those seen in training. Progress is hampered by the difficulty of evaluating such generalization, since a priori, it is rarely clear what makes an instance hard. We study how this issue can be addressed by using large language models (LLMs) to automate benchmark generation, learning to produce increasingly challenging instances in an end-to-end manner. Concretely, given a world parametrized by Datalog rules, and an Edge Transformer as the reasoning evaluator, we use LLM-driven evolutionary search (based on FunSearch) and autonomous agentic search to discover sampling functions that yield hard problem instances. We also show that the Edge Transformer can be improved using this data such that it generalizes well to further data perturbations. Finally, we show that the same machinery can be applied to novel worlds proposed by LLMs, opening the door to autonomous research on neural relational reasoning.

AI.tldr: LLMs are used to automatically generate increasingly challenging relational reasoning benchmarks through evolutionary search, improving neural models' systematic generalization to harder instances.
AI.motivation: Neural models struggle with systematic generalization in relational reasoning, especially when applying learned knowledge to instances harder than those seen in training. Evaluation is difficult because it's unclear what makes instances hard a priori.
AI.method: The approach uses LLMs to automate benchmark generation through LLM-driven evolutionary search (FunSearch) and autonomous agentic search to discover sampling functions that produce increasingly hard problem instances in Datalog-parametrized worlds, using Edge Transformer as evaluator.
AI.result: Edge Transformer can be improved using this generated data to generalize better to further data perturbations. The same approach works on novel worlds proposed by LLMs.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven automated benchmark generation enables systematic study of neural relational reasoning generalization and opens the door to autonomous research in this domain.

title: From Meta Idea to Advanced Mathematical Discovery -- Human-AI Co-Discovery of Sign-Embedding Quantum Algorithms
summary: AI-assisted mathematics is often evaluated on solving predefined problems. In practice, however, many important advances begin earlier, when a vague research intuition is transformed into a concrete problem, a promising route, and a theorem family worth proving. This report studies that stage through a case study that led to sign-embedding quantum algorithms for matrix equations and matrix functions, foundational primitives in quantum linear algebra and operator-output quantum algorithms. The project began with a human-originated intuition that rational approximation is especially effective for jump-type functions such as the sign function, and might therefore serve as a design principle for quantum algorithms. Rather than merely assisting after the problem was fixed, AI-assisted exploration, including workflows later integrated into the agentic AI-mathematician system AIM, played a key role in expanding this intuition into a route map, comparing candidate formulations, and converging toward sign embedding as the central framework. AIM then helped connect a known matrix-sign identity to wider classes of matrix equations and matrix functions, and drafted proof and complexity calculations. The decisive scientific judgments remained human: selecting which human-AI-expanded routes were worth pursuing, rejecting a Cayley-trapezoidal approximation when its validity required a hidden condition, and refining the Sylvester implementation from a coarse quadratic-gap query route to the final factorized and scaled analysis. The report argues that human-AI co-discovery workflows, with systems such as AIM as important components, are most valuable not as standalone theorem provers, but as research partners for problem formation, connection discovery, derivation, and skeptical review inside a human-gated research loop.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a case study of human-AI collaborative mathematics where AI systems (specifically AIM) helped transform a human intuition about rational approximation for jump functions into concrete quantum algorithms for matrix equations and functions, with humans maintaining final scientific judgment throughout the research process.
AI.motivation: Current AI-assisted mathematics evaluation focuses too narrowly on solving predefined problems, missing the crucial early stage where vague research intuitions are transformed into concrete problems and promising routes. The paper aims to study this formative stage through a real case study.
AI.method: Case study methodology examining a research project that led to sign-embedding quantum algorithms. Uses the AIM (agentic AI-mathematician) system as a research partner to explore intuitions, compare formulations, discover connections, and draft proofs while keeping humans in the judgment loop.
AI.result: Successfully developed sign-embedding quantum algorithms for matrix equations and matrix functions, foundational primitives in quantum linear algebra. Demonstrated how AI-assisted exploration expanded a human intuition about rational approximation for jump functions into a concrete research roadmap and helped connect a known matrix-sign identity to wider classes of problems.
AI.conclusion: Human-AI co-discovery workflows are most valuable not as standalone theorem provers but as research partners for problem formation, connection discovery, derivation, and skeptical review within a human-gated research loop, where humans maintain decisive scientific judgment while AI systems enhance exploratory capabilities.

title: Agentic Knowledge Tracing: A Multi-Agent LLM Architecture for Stealth Assessment of Financial Literacy in Serious Games
summary: Assessing financial literacy during gameplay without disrupting the learning experience remains a key challenge in serious games for education. We present the Agentic BKT pipeline, a multi-agent large language model architecture for stealth assessment of financial competencies from open-ended gameplay events. The pipeline processes events from a 2D platformer serious game aligned with the OECD/INFE financial literacy framework through four phases: (1) the game captures every player decision as a structured event log; (2) an LLM event classifier labels each action on a four-point rubric validated against three domain experts (Fleiss kappa = 0.624, substantial agreement); (3) four domain-specific agents specializing in risk mitigation, investing, spending, and credit management perform session-level reasoning over behavioral trajectories, feeding per-competency Bayesian Knowledge Tracing that estimates mastery within each domain; and (4) an expert judge agent synthesizes the domain-level estimates into an overall mastery score. Evaluated with 193 K-12 participants across 264 game sessions, the Agentic BKT pipeline yields mastery estimates significantly correlated with learning gain (r = 0.276, p = 0.0001) and post-test scores (r = 0.333, p < 0.0001) while showing no correlation with pre-test scores, providing both convergent and discriminant validity. The multi-agent approach approximately triples the predictive validity of a single-LLM baseline (r = 0.095, not significant) in this study, demonstrating that domain decomposition and session-level reasoning play a central role in capturing the multidimensional nature of financial literacy from gameplay

AI.tldr: Agentic BKT pipeline uses multi-agent LLM architecture to stealthily assess financial literacy from gameplay, showing significantly better predictive validity than single-LLM baselines.
AI.motivation: Assessing financial literacy during gameplay without disrupting the learning experience is challenging. Current approaches lack effective stealth assessment methods that can accurately measure competencies from open-ended gameplay while maintaining engagement.
AI.method: Four-phase pipeline: (1) capture player decisions as structured event logs from a 2D platformer serious game; (2) use LLM event classifier to label actions on validated rubric; (3) deploy four domain-specific agents for session-level reasoning feeding into Bayesian Knowledge Tracing per competency; (4) expert judge agent synthesizes domain estimates into overall mastery score.
AI.result: Mastery estimates significantly correlated with learning gain (r=0.276, p=0.0001) and post-test scores (r=0.333, p<0.0001), with no correlation to pre-test scores. Multi-agent approach triples predictive validity compared to single-LLM baseline (r=0.095, NS), demonstrating convergent and discriminant validity.
AI.conclusion: The Agentic BKT pipeline effectively addresses stealth assessment challenges for financial literacy education, showing that domain decomposition and session-level reasoning are crucial for capturing multidimensional competencies from gameplay data.

title: Diagnosing and Mitigating Compounding Failures in Agentic Persuasion via Taxonomic Strategy Retrieval
summary:
Foundation-model agents in multi-step, open-ended environments frequently suffer from compounding errors, where early mistakes contaminate long-horizon trajectories. While Multi-Agent Debate (MAD) succeeds in deterministic domains, agents in subjective tasks like persuasion experience severe problem drift and sycophantic conformity. We identify semantic leakage in standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) as a reproducible trigger for these failures, as standard RAG prioritizes vocabulary overlap over logical necessity.
  To eliminate this leakage, we introduce Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG), a systems intervention that routes strategies through a discrete categorical bottleneck to decouple argumentative structure from topical content. Zero-shot, cross-domain evaluations demonstrate that TS-RAG significantly improves the transfer of abstract logic where standard semantic retrieval collapses. Crucially, TS-RAG acts as a "capability bridge" in asymmetric deployments, empowering lightweight persuaders to consistently defeat parametrically superior opponents (improving win rates from 70.5 to 78.5) and accelerating argumentative efficiency. Finally, we introduce trace-level diagnostics via a turn-by-turn Debate State Representation (DSR), demonstrating the necessity of strict constraints to prevent evaluation collapse via default agentic sycophancy.

AI.tldr: TS-RAG improves multi-agent debate in subjective tasks by eliminating semantic leakage through taxonomic routing, enabling lightweight agents to beat stronger opponents and improving argumentative efficiency.
AI.motivation: Foundation-model agents in open-ended environments suffer from compounding errors, especially in subjective tasks like persuasion where standard multi-agent debate approaches fail due to problem drift, sycophantic conformity, and semantic leakage in standard RAG systems.
AI.method: The paper introduces Taxonomic Strategy RAG (TS-RAG), which routes strategies through a discrete categorical bottleneck to decouple argumentative structure from topical content, eliminating semantic leakage. Also introduces Debate State Representation (DSR) for trace-level diagnostics.
AI.result: TS-RAG significantly improves abstract logic transfer where standard semantic retrieval collapses, empowers lightweight persuaders to consistently defeat parametrically superior opponents (improving win rates from 70.5% to 78.5%), and accelerates argumentative efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Strict constraints via TS-RAG are necessary to prevent evaluation collapse via default agentic sycophancy, and the taxonomic bottleneck approach effectively bridges capability gaps in asymmetric deployments while improving reasoning transfer.

title: On-Device Neural Architecture Search
summary: This paper proposes a new approach to near-sensor computing, in which a lightweight Neural Architecture Search (NAS) is performed directly on the deployment device to find the best tiny neural architecture for analyzing the real-time data acquired through sensors. This new adaptation capability can be particularly useful in the case of human-machine interfaces for which the neural network analyzing the biometrical data can be re-designed each time the user changes, after a guided data collection procedure, fighting the typical data variations between individuals on a new level. To implement the proposed approach a new NAS has been designed and then validated on the Italian Sign Language dataset (ISL), a collection of surface electromyography (sEMG) signals of the signs of the Italian alphabet, using several embedded systems. Moreover, further validation on the Case Western Reserve University dataset (CWRU), a benchmark for intelligent fault diagnosis, is presented to suggest another possible application of the proposed approach. When run on a Raspberry Pi 4, the proposed NAS performs beyond the state of the art proposing a tiny neural architecture having 0.63 times less RAM occupancy and 5.96 percentage points of more accuracy in the case of the ISL dataset; and 0.44 times less RAM occupancy and 0.2 percentage points of more accuracy in the case of the CWRU dataset.

AI.tldr: A lightweight Neural Architecture Search performed directly on deployment devices enables real-time adaptation of tiny neural networks for sensor data analysis, showing significant improvements in accuracy and resource efficiency.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of data variations between individuals in human-machine interfaces and enable adaptive neural network design for different users without extensive manual tuning, while maintaining efficiency for near-sensor computing on embedded systems.
AI.method: The paper proposes a new lightweight Neural Architecture Search (NAS) approach that runs directly on deployment devices (like Raspberry Pi 4). The NAS searches for optimal tiny neural architectures tailored to real-time sensor data, enabling on-device adaptation without external computational resources.
AI.result: On Raspberry Pi 4, the proposed NAS outperforms state-of-the-art methods: for ISL dataset (sign language recognition), it achieves 5.96% higher accuracy with 0.63x less RAM; for CWRU dataset (fault diagnosis), it achieves 0.2% higher accuracy with 0.44x less RAM.
AI.conclusion: On-device lightweight NAS enables efficient, adaptive neural network design for near-sensor computing, particularly valuable for personalized human-machine interfaces and embedded fault diagnosis applications, demonstrating significant improvements in both accuracy and resource efficiency.

title: Low Variance Trust Region Optimization with Independent Actors and Sequential Updates in Cooperative Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning assumes each agent shares the same reward function and can be trained effectively using the Trust Region framework of single-agent. Instead of relying on other agents' actions, the independent actors setting considers each agent to act based only on its local information, thus having more flexible applications. However, in the sequential update framework, it is required to re-estimate the joint advantage function after each individual agent's policy step. Despite the practical success of importance sampling, the updated advantage function suffers from exponentially high variance problems, which likely result in unstable convergence. In this work, we first analyze the high variance advantage both empirically and theoretically. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a clipping objective to control the upper bounds of the advantage fluctuation in sequential updates. With the proposed objective, we provide a monotonic bound with sub-linear convergence to $ε$-Nash Equilibria. We further derive two new practical algorithms using our clipping objective. The experiment results on three popular multi-agent reinforcement learning benchmarks show that our proposed method outperforms the tested baselines in most environments. By carefully analyzing different training settings, our proposed method is highlighted with both stable convergence properties and the desired low advantage variance estimation. For reproducibility purposes, our source code is publicly available at https://github.com/giangbang/Low-Variance-Trust-Region-MARL.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the high variance problem in sequential multi-agent RL updates by introducing a clipping objective to control advantage fluctuations, enabling stable convergence to ε-Nash Equilibria with reduced variance.
AI.motivation: In cooperative multi-agent RL with independent actors (agents acting on local information only), sequential policy updates using importance sampling suffer from exponentially high variance in joint advantage function estimation, leading to unstable convergence despite practical success of the approach.
AI.method: The authors analyze the high variance advantage problem both empirically and theoretically, then introduce a clipping objective to control the upper bounds of advantage fluctuations in sequential updates. They derive two practical algorithms using this clipping objective and validate them on three popular MARL benchmarks.
AI.result: Experiments show the proposed method outperforms tested baselines in most environments, demonstrating both stable convergence properties and desired low advantage variance estimation. The method achieves monotonic bounds with sub-linear convergence to ε-Nash Equilibria.
AI.conclusion: The clipping objective successfully addresses the high variance problem in sequential multi-agent RL updates, enabling stable convergence while maintaining practical applicability in independent actor settings, with publicly available source code for reproducibility.

title: Do vision-language models search like humans? Reasoning tokens as a reaction-time analog in classic visual-search paradigms
summary: Visual search has been one of the most productive paradigms in the study of visual attention: the way reaction time scales with the number of items distinguishes parallel, "pop-out" search from serial, attention-demanding search. I ask whether vision-language models (VLMs) exhibit the same behavioral signatures. I adapt four classic paradigms: feature versus conjunction search, spatial-configuration (T-vs-L) search, enumeration, and the tilted/vertical search asymmetry; and present them to current frontier and mid-tier models. Because a single model call has no reaction time, I use the number of reasoning ("thinking") tokens a model spends per trial as a within-model analog of search effort, and I compare against a large public human benchmark (Wolfe et al., 2010). The models reproduce several human signatures: feature search costs flat effort while conjunction effort climbs with set size; frontier models hold accuracy where mid-tier models collapse to chance; and a resolution control shows the conjunction cost is genuine search rather than difficulty resolving small shapes. They also diverge from humans in informative ways. The target-present effort slope exceeds the target-absent slope, reversing the human ordering; enumeration remains accurate where humans would lose count; and a reasoning model with adaptive deliberation declines to deliberate on detection tasks altogether, so that a single search expresses itself as an effort gradient in one model and as an accuracy cliff in another. I argue that psychophysical paradigms, applied behaviorally, are a sharp and inexpensive probe of machine visual cognition, and that the points of divergence are as informative as the points of agreement.

AI.tldr: VLMs tested on classic visual search paradigms show both human-like behavioral signatures (feature vs. conjunction search differences) and informative divergences (reversed target-present/target-absent effort slopes, superior enumeration).
AI.motivation: To investigate whether vision-language models exhibit the same behavioral signatures as humans in visual search paradigms, applying psychophysical methods as an inexpensive probe of machine visual cognition.
AI.method: Adapted four classic visual search paradigms (feature vs. conjunction search, spatial-configuration search, enumeration, tilted/vertical search asymmetry) to VLMs. Used number of reasoning tokens as an analog of search effort (instead of reaction time), comparing against human benchmark data from Wolfe et al. (2010). Tested both frontier and mid-tier models.
AI.result: VLMs reproduce several human signatures: feature search shows flat effort while conjunction search effort increases with set size; frontier models maintain accuracy where mid-tier models fail. Divergences include: target-present effort slope exceeds target-absent slope (opposite of humans); enumeration remains accurate beyond human counting limits; reasoning models decline deliberation on detection tasks.
AI.conclusion: Psychophysical paradigms applied behaviorally are effective, inexpensive probes of machine visual cognition. Both similarities and divergences from human performance provide valuable insights into how VLMs process visual information.

title: LLM Evolution as an Industry-Scale Ecosystem: A Lifecycle Perspective on Continual Learning
summary: Continual learning capability is critical for Industrial LLMs, as deployed models must be continuously updated to meet evolving requirements and environments, rather than repeatedly retrained from scratch. However, most existing research focuses on improvements on static benchmarks, failing to capture real industrial needs. In this survey, we reformulate Industrial Continual Learning (ICL) for LLMs as a closed-loop update-and-release problem in a versioned ecosystem, where updates propagate hierarchically to industrial, application-specific models and LLM-powered applications, with capability inheritance and transfer across versions and model families. From this ecosystem perspective, we identify three core challenges: repeated adaptation erodes model plasticity, foundation-model upgrades break capability inheritance, and long-term sustainability is constrained by deployment requirements. We then organize the technical landscape of ICL around five lifecycle design principles: preserving plasticity headroom, treating upgrades as capability transfer, enabling trustworthy continual reinforcement learning, making training recipes self-optimizing, and building accountability as a base layer for long-term iteration. For each principle, we synthesize representative technical directions. Finally, we evaluate the maturity of each principle and its technical components via an evidence-based lens, identify key gaps hindering real-world deployment, and outline a practical ICL deployment blueprint and a pathway for feeding industrial realities back into academic research.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey reframing continual learning for industrial LLMs as a closed-loop update-and-release problem in versioned ecosystems, identifying core challenges and proposing five lifecycle design principles for sustainable deployment.
AI.motivation: Current continual learning research focuses on static benchmarks and fails to address real industrial needs, where deployed LLMs must be continuously updated without full retraining to meet evolving requirements and environments in production settings.
AI.method: The paper reformulates Industrial Continual Learning (ICL) as a closed-loop update-and-release problem in versioned ecosystems. It identifies three core challenges and organizes the technical landscape around five lifecycle design principles, evaluating their maturity and proposing a practical deployment blueprint.
AI.result: The survey identifies three key challenges: 1) repeated adaptation eroding model plasticity, 2) foundation-model upgrades breaking capability inheritance, and 3) deployment requirements constraining long-term sustainability. It maps technical solutions to five design principles and evaluates their current maturity.
AI.conclusion: Industrial Continual Learning for LLMs requires an ecosystem perspective with closed-loop lifecycle management. The paper provides a framework for practical deployment, identifies research gaps, and outlines pathways for integrating industrial realities into academic research for sustainable LLM evolution in production environments.

title: Agentic evolution of physically constrained foundation models
comment: 29 pages, 5 main figures and 4 extended data figures
summary: Artificial intelligence increasingly drives automated scientific discovery, yet contemporary generalist agents lack physical grounding, frequently hallucinating hardware-incompatible designs. Here, we present a physically grounded, multi-agent discovery engine that autonomously architects hardware-compliant computing systems. Anchored by an Evolutionary Knowledge Graph structuring past scientific innovations, the framework extracts an "algorithmic Chain-of-Thought" to transform blind stochastic search into directed structural evolution. Applied to the extreme testbed of foundation model deployment, the engine evolved two hardware-aware compression methodologies surpassing human-engineered heuristics: Q-Enhance mitigates long-context accuracy loss in dense models, and MoE-Salient-AQ outperforms state-of-the-art manual sparse Mixture-of-Experts designs by 3.7% at sub-3-bit regimes. Utilizing a bandwidth-efficient Sensitivity Profile, we successfully deployed a massive 235-billion-parameter model onto a constrained dual-A100 server, reducing memory requirements by 75% with a marginal 0.64% accuracy degradation. By transforming unconstrained combinatorial search into knowledge-driven autonomy, this establishes a scalable hardware-software co-design paradigm for machine-driven discovery within strict physical boundaries.

AI.tldr: A physically-grounded multi-agent AI system autonomously designs hardware-aware AI models, achieving superior compression and deployment on constrained hardware.
AI.motivation: Current AI discovery agents lack physical grounding, often hallucinating hardware-incompatible designs, requiring a solution for autonomous hardware-compliant system design within strict physical constraints.
AI.method: Multi-agent discovery engine with Evolutionary Knowledge Graph structuring past innovations, extracting "algorithmic Chain-of-Thought" to transform stochastic search into directed structural evolution, using bandwidth-efficient Sensitivity Profiles.
AI.result: Evolved Q-Enhance method mitigates long-context accuracy loss in dense models; MoE-Salient-AQ outperforms manual sparse Mixture-of-Experts by 3.7% at sub-3-bit; deployed 235B-parameter model on dual-A100 with 75% memory reduction and only 0.64% accuracy loss.
AI.conclusion: Transforms unconstrained combinatorial search into knowledge-driven autonomy, establishing scalable hardware-software co-design paradigm for machine-driven discovery within physical boundaries.

title: Beyond Shapley: Efficient Computation of Asymmetric Shapley Values
summary: We address the problem of explainability in machine learning models through feature attribution methods. In particular, we consider a variant of Shapley values known as Asymmetric Shapley Values (ASV), which enables the incorporation of causal knowledge into model-agnostic explanations through the use of a causal graph. We show that in certain contexts in which the computation of SHAP is $\#P$-hard, the exact computation of ASV can be done in polynomial time. To extend this algorithmic result, we introduce a notion of equivalence classes over the topological orderings of the underlying causal graph, which is useful to reduce the time to compute ASV. In particular, we present a polynomial-time algorithm (in the number of equivalence classes) to compute it whenever the causal graph is a rooted directed tree. Finally, we develop an algorithm for approximating ASV in arbitrary causal DAGs which relies on a procedure to sample topological orderings uniformly at random. To implement this sampling mechanism we leverage known algorithms as well as simpler alternatives. Our experimental results demonstrate the practical viability of the proposed approach in realistic causal structures.

AI.tldr: ASV incorporates causal knowledge into model explanations, enabling polynomial-time computation in certain cases where SHAP is computationally hard, with algorithms for efficient computation in causal trees and approximation in arbitrary DAGs.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the computational challenges of feature attribution methods in machine learning explainability, particularly the #P-hardness of SHAP computation in certain contexts, and seeks to incorporate causal knowledge to create more efficient and meaningful explanations.
AI.method: The authors propose Asymmetric Shapley Values (ASV) that integrate causal knowledge through causal graphs. They introduce equivalence classes over topological orderings of causal graphs to reduce computation time, develop polynomial-time algorithms for rooted directed trees, and create approximation algorithms for arbitrary DAGs using uniform sampling of topological orderings.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that ASV can be computed exactly in polynomial time in contexts where SHAP is #P-hard, presents efficient algorithms for causal trees, and shows experimentally that the proposed approach is practically viable in realistic causal structures.
AI.conclusion: ASV provides a promising alternative to SHAP for causal-aware feature attribution, offering computational efficiency advantages while maintaining explainability, with practical algorithms that work well in real-world causal graph scenarios.

title: A Spectral Phase Diagram for Binary Few-Shot Classification: Intrinsic Dimensionality, Geometric Saturation, and Representational Diagnosis
summary:
Deciding when to stop collecting labeled examples is a fundamental but undertheorized problem in applied machine learning. The saturation index $S(K) = \operatorname{erank}(\widehatΣ_W^{(K)}) / K$ measures the ratio of the effective rank of the pooled within-class sample covariance to the shot count; we prove it falls below a threshold precisely when the covariance estimator is well-concentrated around the population covariance and the linear discriminant has stabilized. The index is computable in $O(d^3)$ time from support features alone, requiring no test labels or trained classifier.
  Evaluated across $N = 246$ doubling-pair observations from seventeen binary tasks and six datasets, sixteen of seventeen tasks have a positive within-task Spearman correlation between $S(K)$ and marginal accuracy gain (median $ρ= 0.811$). The pooled Spearman correlation is $ρ= 0.548$ ($p = 1.1 \times 10^{-20}$, $N = 246$). A three-phase diagram (exploration, transition, saturation) with mean marginal gains of $3.48\%$, $2.40\%$, and $0.82\%$ is supported by all pairwise significance tests ($p \leq 0.008$). As a binary stopping rule, the index achieves AUC $= 0.752$, providing meaningful probabilistic guidance for annotation decisions. Asymptotic effective rank and peak accuracy show no significant monotone relationship across tasks (Spearman $r_s = 0.380$, $p = 0.133$, $N = 17$). A small saturation index paired with low accuracy diagnoses representational inadequacy. All results are for binary classification with a fixed linear classifier; extensions to $N$-way settings and pretrained backbone representations are discussed as future work.

AI.tldr: A saturation index measures when collecting more labeled data stops improving classifier accuracy, providing a practical stopping rule for annotation decisions without requiring test labels.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental but undertheorized problem in applied machine learning: deciding when to stop collecting labeled examples. Current approaches lack theoretical grounding for determining the optimal point where additional labeling provides diminishing returns.
AI.method: The paper introduces a saturation index S(K) = erank(Σ̂_W^(K))/K, which measures the ratio of effective rank of pooled within-class sample covariance to shot count. This index falls below a threshold when covariance estimation stabilizes and linear discriminant analysis converges. The index is computable in O(d³) time using only support features, requiring no test labels or trained classifier.
AI.result: Evaluation across 246 observations from 17 binary tasks shows strong correlation between S(K) and accuracy gains (median ρ=0.811). A three-phase diagram shows decreasing marginal gains: exploration (3.48%), transition (2.40%), saturation (0.82%). As a binary stopping rule, the index achieves AUC=0.752. A small saturation index paired with low accuracy diagnoses representational inadequacy.
AI.conclusion: The saturation index provides theoretically grounded, practical guidance for deciding when to stop collecting labeled data. It works without test labels or trained classifiers, offering meaningful probabilistic guidance for annotation decisions. Future work includes extensions to N-way settings and pretrained backbone representations.

title: The Clinician's Veto: Navigating Trust, Liability, and Uncertainty in Autonomous AI Prescribing
summary: Autonomous AI systems are transitioning from advisory to autonomous roles for medication prescriptions. Recent United States bill H.R. 238 and Utah's prescription-renewal pilot both authorize AI to prescribe medications in an agentic capacity. While some regulatory guidelines suggest aggregate model performance metrics for clearance, they do not require i) calibrated per-prediction confidence for action-gated thresholds, ii) differentiated communication of uncertainty arising from model ignorance (epistemic) versus genuine clinical ambiguity (aleatoric), and iii) inferential transparency at the moment of decision that allows for liability allocation. Here, we present a regulatory and technical argument (tested with a survey of 136 U.S. prescribing clinicians) positioning these as minimum architectural requirements for safe autonomous prescribing. Our results suggest prescribing clinicians i) would not permit autonomous prescribing without a calibrated confidence-based escalation mechanism, ii) preferred a competing-options summary when uncertainty was aleatoric but shifted to abstention when uncertainty was epistemic, and iii) were only willing to accept additional liability when inferential transparency enabled a substantive judgment under acknowledged uncertainty. These findings indicate our recommended architectural features would encourage higher rates of clinician adoption, largely through collapsing much of what "autonomy" conventionally means. A system meeting these requirements would function less as an autonomous agent and more as a heavily supervised decision-support tool. As legislation and state pilots proceed, our technical argument backed by clinician perspectives provides opportunities for regulation to constrain the degree of autonomy ethically granted to AI in prescribing while aligning liability with the institutional actors who control system design and deployment.

AI.tldr: Autonomous prescribing AI needs calibrated confidence scores, differentiated uncertainty communication, and inferential transparency to be safe and adopted by clinicians, transforming it into supervised decision support rather than true autonomy.
AI.motivation: Current AI prescribing regulations lack critical safety features: calibrated confidence thresholds for intervention, differentiation between model ignorance vs clinical ambiguity uncertainty, and transparency for liability allocation, creating unsafe autonomous prescribing systems.
AI.method: Survey of 136 U.S. prescribing clinicians testing regulatory and technical arguments about minimum architectural requirements, examining preferences for escalation mechanisms, uncertainty handling, and liability acceptance with transparency.
AI.result: Clinicians require calibrated confidence-based escalation, prefer different responses to aleatoric (competing options) vs epistemic (abstention) uncertainty, and only accept liability when inferential transparency enables substantive judgment under acknowledged uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: Recommended architectural features encourage clinician adoption by reducing true autonomy, transforming AI into supervised decision support; regulation should constrain autonomy based on these safety requirements and align liability with system designers.

title: When Do Conservation Laws Survive Learned Representations? Certified Horizons for Latent World Models
summary: We ask a representation-learning question about physical world models: when does a conservation law remain certifiable after a model learns a latent representation? A certified horizon bounds -- in advance, from measurable model defects -- how many steps a rollout provably stays on a physical invariant's level set. The key design choice is what is certified: not a learned latent Hamiltonian or a learned scalar witness (a model can conserve either while drifting in true energy), but the decoded physical invariant obtained by decoding the latent state and evaluating the known invariant. Around this object we derive shell-horizon certificates whose budget decomposes into representation, readout, and latent-dynamics defects, with a monotone alignment bridge through which a soft learned witness yields a certified horizon for the decoded invariant, and test them across state, learned-lift, and pixel observations on conservative systems. Conservation certificates can survive learned representation, but not all geometric priors survive equally: hard canonical symplectic structure yields the longest horizons in known phase coordinates yet does not cross a learned chart, whereas a controlled-Lipschitz-aligned soft invariant survives in the learned-representation settings we test; pixel certification is recovered on a readout-stable sub-tube; and the Kepler problem exposes a geometric boundary. The central object is therefore not a latent Hamiltonian, but a decoded physical invariant whose robustness to representation learning can be measured, certified, and falsified.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes shell-horizon certificates that bound how many steps a model rollout stays on a physical invariant's level set after learning a latent representation, focusing on certifying decoded physical invariants rather than latent Hamiltonians.
AI.motivation: To understand when conservation laws remain certifiable after model learns latent representations, and to develop certificates that can bound in advance how many steps a rollout provably stays on a physical invariant's level set from measurable model defects.
AI.method: Design shell-horizon certificates around decoded physical invariants (obtained by decoding latent state and evaluating known invariant), with budget decomposing into representation, readout, and latent-dynamics defects. Uses monotone alignment bridge where soft learned witness yields certified horizon for decoded invariant. Tests across state, learned-lift, and pixel observations on conservative systems.
AI.result: Conservation certificates can survive learned representation but geometric priors don't survive equally: hard canonical symplectic structure yields longest horizons in known coordinates but doesn't cross learned chart, while controlled-Lipschitz-aligned soft invariant survives in learned-representation settings. Pixel certification recovered on readout-stable sub-tube, and Kepler problem reveals geometric boundary.
AI.conclusion: The central object for certification should not be a latent Hamiltonian, but a decoded physical invariant whose robustness to representation learning can be measured, certified, and falsified.

title: TRUSTMEM: Learning Trustworthy Memory Consolidation for LLM Agents with Long-Term Memory
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents rely on long-term memory to support extended interactions and personalized assistance beyond finite context windows. Existing memory agents actively update external memory through generated write, revise, and delete operations, but these updates may omit important information, corrupt existing memory, or introduce unsupported hallucinated content. Once stored, such errors become persistent system-state failures that can affect future reasoning and generation. In this paper, we propose TrustMem, a framework designed to improve the trustworthiness of memory consolidation. TrustMem relies on a Memory Transition Verifier to evaluate the transition process of memory updates in terms of coverage, preservation, and faithfulness. It further constructs preference pairs among candidate updates under the same memory state, enabling preference-guided reinforcement learning to directly optimize memory updating behaviors. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TrustMem improves both memory utility and reliability: it achieves state-of-the-art results across MemoryAgentBench, HaluMem, and the Mem-alpha validation set, improves HaluMem memory extraction by 12.14 F1 points, and reduces transition-level omission, corruption, and hallucination by 40.1\%, 79.1\%, and 50.0\%, respectively, compared with the strongest baseline for each error type.

AI.tldr: TrustMem improves LLM memory reliability by verifying memory transitions and using preference-guided RL to optimize updates, reducing errors by 40-79%.
AI.motivation: Current LLM memory systems suffer from persistent errors - important information can be omitted, existing memory corrupted, or hallucinated content introduced during updates, leading to system-state failures that affect future reasoning.
AI.method: Proposes TrustMem framework with: 1) Memory Transition Verifier that evaluates updates on coverage, preservation, and faithfulness; 2) Preference-guided reinforcement learning that constructs preference pairs among candidate updates and optimizes memory updating behaviors.
AI.result: Achieves SOTA on MemoryAgentBench, HaluMem, and Mem-alpha; improves HaluMem memory extraction by 12.14 F1 points; reduces transition-level omission by 40.1%, corruption by 79.1%, and hallucination by 50.0% compared to strongest baselines.
AI.conclusion: TrustMem significantly improves both memory utility and reliability in LLM agents by ensuring trustworthy memory consolidation through verifiable transitions and optimized update behaviors.

title: Conformal Orbit-Valid Trust Horizons for Equivariant World Models
summary: Learned world models are useful only over horizons on which their rollout error remains controlled. We study trust-horizon certification for latent world models with known group symmetries. Given a one-step latent residual and a finite-time expansion estimate, we form a raw horizon curve and calibrate it with a split-conformal multiplicative factor. On the reproducible audit set, the conformal factor is $γ_α=1.0$: the raw certificate is already conservative under the audit protocol. Across 50 stable audits, we observe zero anti-conservative violations, corresponding to an exact-binomial 95% upper bound of 5.8% on the violation rate. Our main structural result is that exact equivariance transports a calibrated trust-horizon curve over the group orbit: when the environment dynamics, encoder, predictor, action transform, and latent metric satisfy the stated equivariance/invariance conditions, rollout errors and trust horizons are orbit-constant. Empirically, the implemented models exhibit small orbit-transport residuals, with median 1.1% and maximum 4.1% over 14 orbit audits. The certificate is also non-vacuous (median certified-to-measured horizon ratio 0.67). A certificate-level calibration-cost study shows two complementary regimes. On a symmetric 2D substrate, equivariant, plain, and augmented models are all orbit-valid from a single calibration sector -- no separation, because the substrate already makes non-equivariant baselines approximately orbit-robust. A 3D yaw audit shows the other regime: the equivariant model obtains a one-sector safe and non-vacuous orbit-valid certificate, while healthy non-equivariant baselines pay violation, slack, sharpness, or additional-sector cost. The certificate is a conservative, distributional audit rather than a global reachability guarantee, and certificate-guided subgoal spacing is not confirmed in the current 3D CEM-MPC behavior layer.

AI.tldr: Trust-horizon certification for latent world models using conformal methods that leverage group symmetries to provide conservative rollout error guarantees across environment transformations.
AI.motivation: Learned world models are only useful over horizons where their rollout error remains controlled, but current methods lack reliable certification of how far these models can be trusted for planning and control tasks.
AI.method: Proposes trust-horizon certification using conformal prediction with group symmetries: form raw horizon curves from one-step latent residuals and finite-time expansion estimates, calibrate with split-conformal multiplicative factors, and leverage exact equivariance to transport certificates across group orbits.
AI.result: Certificates show zero anti-conservative violations in 50 audits (95% upper bound of 5.8% violation rate), small orbit-transport residuals (median 1.1%, max 4.1%), non-vacuous performance (median certified-to-measured ratio 0.67), and demonstrate regimes where equivariant models obtain efficient certification while non-equivariant baselines pay various costs.
AI.conclusion: The method provides conservative, distributional certification for world model trust horizons that leverages group symmetries for efficient orbit-wide guarantees, though it remains an audit tool rather than a global reachability guarantee and shows limitations in 3D planning applications.

title: Elo-Disentangled Player-Style Embeddings for Human Chess via Rating-Conditioned Residual Move Model
summary: We study representation learning for individual human chess style: a per-player embedding learned from a player's move history such that inner products measure stylistic similarity, while being approximately disentangled from playing strength (Elo). Our key design is a residual formulation: a rating-conditioned base move model (Maia-3 policy logits plus Stockfish-derived features, scored over Maia-2-proposed candidates) captures what a typical player of a given strength would play, and a frozen copy of it anchors a learned move encoder and a per-player vector z, so that z explains only deviations from rating-typical play. The base model improves move prediction over the strong Maia-3 policy by 27-37% relative NLL across the rating spectrum, with the largest gains at the top (2800+); Stockfish's marginal value grows monotonically with Elo (negligible at 900-1200, +0.085 nats at 2800+). On a shared Elo-stratified benchmark of 22,620 held-out decisions, top-1 move-matching rises monotonically from Maia-2 to Maia-3 to the Stockfish-augmented base (0.51 -> 0.57 -> 0.68): the base is +33% relative top-1 over Maia-2 and +19% over Maia-3 (30% lower NLL), with the engine-feature lift largest at high Elo. The player embedding adds little to raw move-matching on top of this base -- its marginal top-1 gain falls within the 95% confidence interval -- and its value is instead representational: z generalizes to held-out decisions without overfitting, re-identifies players from disjoint games above chance, and a linear probe recovers rating from z with only R^2 = 0.06 (no better nonlinearly), evidence it captures style on an Elo-orthogonal axis. We argue that a strong rating-conditioned base plus a compact, Elo-disentangled embedding -- separating typical play from individual deviation -- is an economical, interpretable model of individual style, an alternative to per-player preference fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a residual formulation for learning human chess style embeddings that are disentangled from playing strength, using a rating-conditioned base model plus per-player vectors that capture stylistic deviations.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to develop a method for learning individual human chess style representations that are disentangled from playing strength (Elo rating), addressing the challenge of separating stylistic preferences from ability level in chess move analysis.
AI.method: The key design is a residual formulation: a rating-conditioned base model captures what typical players of given strength would play, while a frozen copy of it anchors a learned move encoder and per-player vector z that explains only deviations from rating-typical play. The base model combines Maia-3 policy logits with Stockfish-derived features scored over Maia-2-proposed candidates.
AI.result: The base model improves move prediction by 27-37% relative NLL across ratings, with largest gains at high Elo. The player embedding generalizes to held-out decisions without overfitting, re-identifies players above chance, and a linear probe shows R^2 = 0.06 for rating prediction, indicating Elo-orthogonal style capture.
AI.conclusion: A strong rating-conditioned base plus compact Elo-disentangled embedding provides an economical, interpretable model of individual chess style as an alternative to per-player fine-tuning, successfully separating typical play from individual deviation.

title: Supervised Reinforcement Learning for the Coordination of Distributed Energy Resources
summary: The increasing integration of distributed energy resources (DERs) is crucial for power system decarbonization, yet unlocking DERs' flexibility is challenged by their inherent uncertainties and modelling complexity. As traditional optimization methods struggle with such uncertainty and complexity of DERs, reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a promising alternative for DER management. However, standard RL methods suffer from sample inefficiency and sub-optimality when trained from scratch. Inspired by the training paradigms in large language models, this paper proposes a Supervised Reinforcement Learning (SRL) framework for learning DER coordination policies. This framework first pre-trains a policy on demonstration data in a supervised-learning fashion, which is then further fine-tuned using RL. Furthermore, we propose a two-step fine-tuning process: offline fine-tuning for enhancing policy performance and online fine-tuning for adapting it to the real-world dynamics. Experiments demonstrate that RL implementations based on the proposed framework significantly outperform all benchmarks, achieving high cost efficiency even under low-quality demonstration data.

AI.tldr: A Supervised Reinforcement Learning framework combines supervised pre-training on demonstration data with reinforcement learning fine-tuning for improved distributed energy resource management.
AI.motivation: Distributed energy resources (DERs) have uncertainties and modeling complexity that challenge traditional optimization methods, while standard reinforcement learning suffers from sample inefficiency and sub-optimality when trained from scratch.
AI.method: Proposed a Supervised Reinforcement Learning (SRL) framework that first pre-trains policies on demonstration data using supervised learning, then fine-tunes with RL. Includes a two-step fine-tuning process: offline for performance enhancement and online for real-world adaptation.
AI.result: RL implementations based on the proposed framework significantly outperform all benchmarks, achieving high cost efficiency even with low-quality demonstration data.
AI.conclusion: The SRL framework effectively combines the strengths of supervised learning and reinforcement learning to overcome sample inefficiency in DER coordination, offering a promising approach for power system decarbonization through improved DER management.

title: Transferability for General Reasoning: An Automated Curriculum for Multi-Domain RLVR
comment: 32 pages, including supplementary material; code available at https://github.com/YangYongJin/transfer-aware-curriculum
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has been extended from single-domain training to multi-domain reasoning suites spanning mathematics, programming, and science. However, the training curriculum (how often each domain is sampled) is typically fixed or hand-tuned, even though reasoning skills transfer unevenly across domains. Existing learnability-based curricula adapt to where the policy is currently improving, but are blind to whether a gradient step on the selected domain benefits the remaining domains. In this paper, we propose Transfer-Aware Curriculum (TAC), a bandit-style online curriculum that prioritizes domains whose updates broadly benefit the rest of the training suite. TAC repurposes signals already produced by RL training: per-domain advantages capture local learnability, and projected gradients, taken from the GRPO step being computed, estimate cross-domain transferability via gradient-geometry alignment, at negligible cost (<1% wall-clock overhead). Across a six-domain reasoning suite, TAC achieves the best macro-averaged accuracy on both Qwen3-1.7B and Llama3.2-3B, outperforming proportional random sampling, a hand-designed schedule, and a learnability-only bandit, and improving over the last of these by up to 2.8 points (10% relative). Ablations show performance degrades sharply when the transferability term is removed, and TAC remains robust on imbalanced training mixtures where learnability-only curricula over-commit to dominant domains. Our findings establish cross-domain transferability as a key signal for curriculum design in multi-domain RLVR.

AI.tldr: TAC (Transfer-Aware Curriculum) is a bandit-style online curriculum method for multi-domain RLVR that prioritizes domains based on both local learnability and cross-domain transferability, achieving significant performance improvements over existing curriculum methods.
AI.motivation: Existing curriculum methods for multi-domain reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) are either fixed, hand-tuned, or only consider local learnability. They fail to account for whether training on one domain benefits other domains, even though reasoning skills transfer unevenly across different domains like mathematics, programming, and science.
AI.method: Proposes TAC (Transfer-Aware Curriculum), a bandit-style online curriculum that uses two signals: 1) per-domain advantages (from RL training) to capture local learnability, and 2) projected gradients from GRPO steps to estimate cross-domain transferability via gradient-geometry alignment. This approach adds minimal computational overhead (<1% wall-clock).
AI.result: TAC achieves best macro-averaged accuracy on six-domain reasoning suite with both Qwen3-1.7B and Llama3.2-3B models, outperforming proportional random sampling, hand-designed schedules, and learnability-only bandits by up to 2.8 points (10% relative improvement). Ablations show performance degrades without transferability term, and TAC remains robust on imbalanced training mixtures.
AI.conclusion: Cross-domain transferability is a key signal for curriculum design in multi-domain RLVR, and TAC effectively leverages both local learnability and transferability signals to create more effective training curricula.

title: Holographic Memory for Zero-Shot Compositional Reasoning in Knowledge Graphs: A Mechanistic Study of Where and Why It Fails
comment: 15 pages, 5 figures, 5 tables. Code available at https://github.com/iamhero2709/holographic-memory
summary:
Knowledge graph embedding (KGE) models predict single-hop links well but have no mechanism for zero-shot compositional queries: multi-hop questions whose relation chains never appeared during training. Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR), which bind and unbind symbols via circular convolution, are a theoretically attractive candidate, since binding is approximately invertible and associative. We test whether this promise holds.
  We study two holographic memory variants, real-valued HRR and phase-only Fourier HRR (FHRR), each with a modern Hopfield cleanup, on FB15k-237 over five seeds. Four findings follow. First, both are competitive single-hop retrievers (filtered MRR 0.358 +/- 0.002 for HRR, 0.350 +/- 0.021 for FHRR). Second, neither composes zero-shot: accuracy stays at chance across all cleanup temperatures. Third, the main contribution, we localise the failure mechanistically. A hop-1 probe shows the memory recovers the correct intermediate entity with high fidelity (MRR 0.896 +/- 0.002 for HRR), yet composition still fails even with a verified-correct intermediate. A second probe shows why: posing the ground-truth second-hop fact as a standalone atomic query, bypassing composition entirely, already recovers it at only 0.26 to 0.48x average atomic accuracy, uniformly across relation fan-out. The bottleneck is not the bind-unbind algebra or the cleanup; it is that facts compositional chains pass through are intrinsically harder for the superposed memory to retrieve, a capacity and interference effect present already at a single hop. Fourth, we prove (Lemma 4.1) that FHRR's softmax cleanup is not phase-equivariant, compounding the primary failure on the minority of chains where hop-1 itself errs. Fixing zero-shot composition requires improving retrieval capacity under superposition, not just redesigning the cleanup.

AI.tldr: Modern holographic memories (HRR/FHRR) with Hopfield cleanup perform well on single-hop queries but fail at zero-shot compositional queries despite good intermediate entity retrieval - the bottleneck is retrieval capacity under superposition, not the bind-unbind algebra.
AI.motivation: Knowledge graph embedding models handle single-hop links well but lack mechanisms for zero-shot compositional queries (multi-hop questions with unseen relation chains during training). Holographic Reduced Representations (HRR) offer theoretical promise due to approximately invertible and associative binding via circular convolution, but their practical viability needs testing.
AI.method: Study two holographic memory variants (real-valued HRR and phase-only Fourier HRR) each with modern Hopfield cleanup on FB15k-237 dataset over five seeds. Investigate performance on single-hop retrieval and zero-shot composition, using mechanistic probes to understand failure modes, including hop-1 intermediate entity recovery and standalone atomic query tests.
AI.result: 1) Both HRR variants are competitive single-hop retrievers (MRR ~0.35-0.358). 2) Neither composes zero-shot - accuracy stays at chance. 3) Hop-1 probes show memory recovers correct intermediate entities with high fidelity (MRR ~0.896), yet composition still fails. 4) Even posing ground-truth second-hop facts as standalone atomic queries yields only 0.26-0.48x average atomic accuracy, showing facts in compositional chains are intrinsically harder to retrieve due to superposition capacity/interference effects.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot composition failure stems from retrieval capacity limitations under superposition, not the bind-unbind algebra or cleanup (though FHRR's cleanup has additional non-equivariance issues).
