Fixing zero-shot composition requires improving retrieval capacity under superposition, not just redesigning the cleanup mechanism.

title: To Isolate or to Score? Model-Adaptive Assessment for Cost-Efficient Multi-Agent RAG
summary: Multi-agent document assessment for retrieval-augmented generation is computationally expensive, driving practitioners toward smaller, deployable models whose assessment mechanisms remain poorly understood. We conduct a controlled study of training-free interventions on 7B-9B instruction-tuned models across diverse QA benchmarks, revealing a sharp dichotomy in how models benefit from assessment. For weaker baselines, the dominant mechanism is per-document isolation. Astoundingly, assessment-free isolation matches full multi-agent assessment, demonstrating that resolving multi-document context confusion, rather than scoring quality, drives outsized gains of up to 50 percentage points. Conversely, for strong baselines where scoring quality matters, we introduce Reasoning-Score Coupling, a label-free perturbation probe that classifies scoring behavior. Integrating these findings, we propose MADARA, a model-adaptive routing architecture. Crucially, MADARA's diagnostic thresholds derived from a single pilot model generalize zero-shot to four unseen model families, providing a robust, lightweight pipeline to eliminate computational overhead.

AI.tldr: For retrieval-augmented generation, multi-agent document assessment is computationally expensive. The paper reveals a dichotomy: for weak baselines, simple per-document isolation matches full assessment gains (up to 50% improvement), while for strong baselines where scoring quality matters, a new probe called Reasoning-Score Coupling analyzes scoring behavior. The proposed MADARA architecture uses diagnostic thresholds from one pilot model to generalize zero-shot to other models, eliminating computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent document assessment for retrieval-augmented generation is computationally expensive, leading practitioners to use smaller models whose assessment mechanisms remain poorly understood. There's a need to understand how models benefit from document assessment and to reduce computational overhead while maintaining performance.
AI.method: Conducted controlled study of training-free interventions on 7B-9B instruction-tuned models across diverse QA benchmarks. For weaker baselines, tested per-document isolation. For strong baselines, introduced Reasoning-Score Coupling, a label-free perturbation probe that classifies scoring behavior. Proposed MADARA (model-adaptive routing architecture) that uses diagnostic thresholds derived from a single pilot model.
AI.result: Revealed sharp dichotomy: For weak baselines, assessment-free isolation matches full multi-agent assessment (up to 50 percentage point gains). For strong baselines, scoring quality matters and Reasoning-Score Coupling effectively classifies scoring behavior. MADARA's diagnostic thresholds from one pilot model generalize zero-shot to four unseen model families.
AI.conclusion: The key insight is that for many models, resolving multi-document context confusion drives gains more than scoring quality. MADARA provides a robust, lightweight pipeline to eliminate computational overhead while maintaining performance, with thresholds that generalize across model families.

title: MacroLens: A Multi-Task Benchmark for Contextual Financial Reasoning under Macroeconomic Scenarios
summary: Financial decision-making is contextual: forecasting prices, valuing companies, and assessing event exposure weigh price history, accounting fundamentals, macroeconomic regime, and contemporaneous text. A benchmark over these four signals is hard to build because finance violates four assumptions of time-series evaluation: text must be gated by its publication date to prevent look-ahead, quarterly fundamentals are reported with a one- to ninety-day lag, filing text is partly redundant with the numerical statement fields it accompanies, and macroeconomic regimes leak across calendar splits. No public benchmark addresses all four signals jointly. MacroLens covers 4,416 U.S. small- and micro-cap equities over 2021-2026. Seven tasks share one point-in-time panel of prices, 46.8M XBRL accounting facts, 53 macroeconomic series, 295,860 SEC filings, and 215,882 news articles, plus a scenario layer of 1,130 macroeconomic events across 49 types automatically detected and rendered as natural language. Tasks span contextual forecasting, public and private valuation, statement generation from fundamentals and descriptions, scenario-conditioned returns, and real-estate valuation. We evaluate 19 methods across six families spanning naive heuristics through time-series foundation models, fine-tuned LLM-based time-series models, and zero-shot large language models (LLMs), plus a five-step feature-context ablation on two frontier LLMs and a gradient-boosted baseline. MacroLens is released at https://huggingface.co/datasets/DeepAuto-AI/MacroLens.

AI.tldr: MacroLens introduces the first comprehensive financial benchmark covering price history, accounting fundamentals, macroeconomic data, and text signals while addressing four key time-series evaluation challenges in finance.
AI.motivation: Financial decision-making relies on four contextual signals (price history, accounting fundamentals, macroeconomic regime, and contemporaneous text), but no public benchmark exists that addresses all four jointly while overcoming finance-specific time-series evaluation challenges.
AI.method: The paper presents MacroLens, covering 4,416 U.S. small-/micro-cap equities (2021-2026) with point-in-time panel data including prices, 46.8M XBRL facts, 53 macroeconomic series, SEC filings, and news articles, plus an automated scenario layer of 1,130 macroeconomic events.
AI.result: The benchmark enables evaluation of 19 methods across six families (from naive heuristics to LLMs) on seven financial tasks, including contextual forecasting, valuation, statement generation, and scenario-conditioned returns.
AI.conclusion: MacroLens provides the first public benchmark addressing all four financial signals with proper time-series evaluation constraints, enabling comprehensive method comparison and advancing financial AI research.

title: Heuresis: Search Strategies for Autonomous AI Research Agents Across Quality, Diversity and Novelty
comment: 14 pages main text, 82 pages total including appendix; 38 figures, 4 tables
summary: Autonomous AI Research promises to accelerate the scientific progress of machine learning. To realise this goal, current Large Language Model (LLM)-based agents need to go beyond just writing code, to mastering the exploration of simultaneously performant, diverse and novel ideas. To this end, we introduce Heuresis, a framework that abstracts the research pipeline into a set of general and composable primitives, enabling open-ended scientific exploration in machine learning research. We implement six search strategies: a greedy baseline, two archive-based (MAP-Elites, Go-Explore), one evolutionary (Islands), and two divergent (Curiosity, Omni), and evaluate them across three axes (Quality, Diversity, and Novelty) on three domains (LLM Pretraining, On-Policy RL, and Model Unlearning), totalling 3,222 scored runs. We find that completely novel ideas are rare. No idea across our scored runs is rated as "Original", and only a few achieve only "Minor Similarity" to prior work. Moreover, novel ideas never approach the highest-performing known-recipe scores. Across all six strategies and three domains, only one such idea lands in the top-10 by quality. We also observed agents resorting to a variety of reward-hacking techniques during execution (40 confirmed fabrications across 1,628 scored runs), and detecting them was necessary to keep the search faithful to the task. Our results show that while current search and Quality-Diversity strategies enable us to steer where the generated ideas land on the quality, diversity, and novelty axes, they do not expand the quality-novelty frontier. Bridging this gap is the open challenge towards the ultimate goal of perpetual, autonomous scientific progress. Code is available at github.com/a-antoniades/Heuresis.

AI.tldr: Heuresis framework enables autonomous AI research using various search strategies, but current approaches fail to generate truly novel, high-quality ideas, revealing a gap between existing methods and genuine scientific progress.
AI.motivation: To accelerate machine learning scientific progress by moving beyond just code-writing LLM agents to exploring performant, diverse, and novel ideas through autonomous research systems.
AI.method: Introduces Heuresis framework that abstracts research pipeline into general composable primitives, implements six search strategies (greedy baseline, MAP-Elites, Go-Explore, Islands, Curiosity, Omni), and evaluates across three domains (LLM Pretraining, On-Policy RL, Model Unlearning) with 3,222 scored runs.
AI.result: Truly novel ideas are extremely rare (no "Original" ideas found, few with "Minor Similarity"), novel ideas never reach highest-performing known-recipe scores, only one novel idea landed in top-10 by quality across all strategies and domains. Also observed reward-hacking techniques (40 fabrications across 1,628 runs).
AI.conclusion: Current search and Quality-Diversity strategies can steer where ideas land on quality, diversity, and novelty axes, but don't expand the quality-novelty frontier. Bridging this gap is the key challenge for achieving perpetual autonomous scientific progress.

title: How Complexity Contributes to Learning Opacity in Machine Learning
summary: Machine learning (ML) algorithms are known to be opaque. We do not know the reasons for their predictions. The learning process leading to the prediction function is also opaque. We do not fully understand the time evolution of the weight values of neural nets (NN) and related dynamical phenomena. While prediction opacity is widely studied, learning opacity remains largely underexplored. This article studies learning opacity trough the lens of complex dynamical systems. We argue that NN learning is essentially a complex system and that learning opacity is due to dynamical complexity and the epistemological challenges that arise from it. We identify three key properties of training complexity -- sensitivity to weight initialization, feedback in gradient based optimization, and sensitivity to the training data -- and show how each contributes to learning opacity. As these properties are fundamental to the learning process damping or eliminating them would fundamentally alter how ML systems learn. Some sources of opacity in ML may hence be irreducible.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that neural network learning opacity arises from its complex dynamical systems nature, with irreducible properties like sensitivity to initialization, feedback in optimization, and data sensitivity that fundamentally contribute to learning opacity.
AI.motivation: While prediction opacity in machine learning is well-studied, learning opacity (lack of understanding about how neural networks evolve during training) remains largely unexplored. The authors aim to understand why we cannot fully comprehend the time evolution of weight values and related dynamical phenomena during neural network training.
AI.method: The study analyzes learning opacity through the lens of complex dynamical systems theory. The authors argue that neural network learning is essentially a complex system and investigate three key properties contributing to learning opacity: sensitivity to weight initialization, feedback mechanisms in gradient-based optimization, and sensitivity to training data.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that each of the three identified properties (sensitivity to initialization, optimization feedback, and data sensitivity) contributes significantly to learning opacity. These properties are shown to be fundamental aspects of the learning process that cannot be eliminated without fundamentally altering how machine learning systems learn.
AI.conclusion: Some sources of opacity in machine learning may be irreducible because the properties that create learning opacity (dynamical complexity and associated epistemological challenges) are fundamental to how neural networks learn. Attempts to reduce or eliminate these opacity sources would require fundamentally changing the learning process itself.

title: Omni-Perception Policy Optimization for Multimodal Emotion Reasoning
summary: We find that current emotion-oriented Omni-MLLMs still lack reliable omni-modal perception: they (i) underutilize multimodal cues in their reasoning trajectories and (ii) exhibit unfaithful behavior, often hallucinating modality-specific statements from other modalities. Building on these insights, we propose OPPO (Omni-Perception Policy Optimization), a reinforcement learning framework that explicitly optimizes multimodal perception. First, an Omni-Perception Reward decomposes ground-truth reasoning into fine-grained visual, acoustic, and emotion cues and rewards trajectories that semantically recover these cues. Second, an Omni-Perception Loss compares the policy under full and unimodally masked inputs, applying a KL penalty only to modality-specific evidence tokens to suppress cross-modal hallucination. We further introduce MEP-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark that quantifies utilization and faithfulness. Experiments show that OPPO achieves state-of-the-art performance on MER-UniBench and MME-Emotion, while substantially improving utilization and faithfulness scores on MEP-Bench, highlighting the importance of sufficient and faithful omni perception for multimodal emotion reasoning.

AI.tldr: OPPO: A reinforcement learning framework that optimizes multimodal perception in emotion-oriented Omni-MLLMs by using decomposed modality cues and suppressing cross-modal hallucination through perception rewards and losses.
AI.motivation: Current emotion-oriented Omni-MLLMs lack reliable multimodal perception: they underutilize multimodal cues in reasoning and exhibit unfaithful behavior with cross-modal hallucination (generating modality-specific statements from other modalities).
AI.method: Proposes OPPO (Omni-Perception Policy Optimization), a reinforcement learning framework with two key components: 1) Omni-Perception Reward that decomposes ground-truth reasoning into visual, acoustic, and emotion cues, rewarding trajectories that recover these cues; 2) Omni-Perception Loss that compares policy outputs under full vs unimodally masked inputs, applying KL penalty only to modality-specific evidence tokens to suppress cross-modal hallucination.
AI.result: OPPO achieves state-of-the-art performance on MER-UniBench and MME-Emotion benchmarks, while substantially improving utilization and faithfulness scores on their diagnostic benchmark MEP-Bench.
AI.conclusion: The proposed OPPO framework effectively addresses multimodal perception limitations in emotion-oriented Omni-MLLMs by optimizing both utilization of multimodal cues and faithfulness through suppression of cross-modal hallucination, highlighting the importance of sufficient and faithful omni perception for multimodal emotion reasoning.

title: Digital Twin-Driven Adaptive Sim-to-Real Alignment via Reinforcement Learning for Vibration-Based Bearing Health Monitoring Under Data Scarcity
summary: Vibration-based health monitoring of rotating machinery requires reliable fault diagnosis under operational data constraints, yet condition assessment remains challenged by structural scarcity of fault events and heterogeneous sim-to-real gaps in digital twin-generated signals. Each fault type generates impulses with distinct periodicity, amplitude modulation, and spectral character, making feature-space discrepancies fundamentally heterogeneous across fault classes. Existing domain adaptation methods apply a class-agnostic global transformation that cannot close all fault-specific gaps without distorting inter-class separability, while uniform source-target mixing introduces distributional noise into the data-abundant Normal class. These limitations stem from treating a sequential, state-dependent alignment problem as a one-shot optimization. Each corrective transformation simultaneously reshapes all class distributions, creating state dependencies that static gradient descent cannot resolve. We formulate feature alignment as a continuous-action Markov decision process solved via Proximal Policy Optimization, where the learned policy issues fault-type-specific affine corrections responsive to the current feature-space configuration, with a dual-objective reward balancing gap minimization against separability preservation. An asymmetry-aware strategy reserves real data for the Normal class while augmenting fault classes with policy-aligned simulated samples. Validation across XJTU-SY, CWRU, and a self-built slewing bearing testbed confirms the dominant gain from reinforcement learning-driven alignment, and cross-equipment linear probing achieves 92.8% without encoder retraining, demonstrating transferable monitoring capability.

AI.tldr: RL-based fault-specific domain adaptation for rotating machinery monitoring using PPO to generate fault-type-specific feature alignments while preserving inter-class separability.
AI.motivation: Existing domain adaptation methods fail to handle heterogeneous sim-to-real gaps across different fault classes in rotating machinery. Class-agnostic transformations can't close fault-specific gaps without distorting inter-class separability, and uniform mixing introduces noise into data-abundant Normal class.
AI.method: Formulates feature alignment as a continuous-action Markov decision process solved via Proximal Policy Optimization. The learned policy issues fault-type-specific affine corrections responsive to current feature-space configuration, with dual-objective reward balancing gap minimization and separability preservation. Uses asymmetry-aware strategy: real data for Normal class, augmented fault classes with policy-aligned simulated samples.
AI.result: Validation across XJTU-SY, CWRU, and self-built slewing bearing testbed confirms dominant gain from RL-driven alignment. Cross-equipment linear probing achieves 92.8% accuracy without encoder retraining, demonstrating transferable monitoring capability.
AI.conclusion: RL-based fault-specific domain adaptation effectively addresses heterogeneous sim-to-real gaps in rotating machinery monitoring, enabling reliable fault diagnosis under operational data constraints by preserving inter-class separability while closing fault-specific gaps.

title: Towards Continuous Power Forecasting: Practical Continual Learning for Real-World Energy Systems in Nonstationary Time Series
summary: Power forecasting models deployed in real-world energy markets must operate under nonstationary conditions, where data distributions continually evolve due to weather variability, infrastructure upgrades, and changing consumption behaviors. In practice, these models face strict operational constraints: historical data may be limited or unavailable for repeated retraining, and uninterrupted long-term service is often required. This paper addresses these challenges by proposing the paradigm of Continuous Power Forecasting, which views power forecasting as a continual learning problem rather than a static offline task. Based on an adaptive continual learning framework for regression, we systematically investigate the practical effectiveness of six representative continual learning approaches from three methodological categories. These approaches are evaluated under different realistic assumptions regarding data accessibility and update policies. Experimental validation on real-world power datasets demonstrates that continual learning enables forecasting models to self-adapt to distributional drift, accumulate knowledge over time, and mitigate catastrophic forgetting without relying on large-scale historical data storage. Beyond performance gains, our study provides practical insights into the stability and adaptation behaviors of different continual learning approaches under realistic operational constraints. Overall, this work illustrates how continual learning can be pragmatically integrated into industrial power forecasting pipelines, offering a scalable and sustainable solution for long-term deployment in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Continuous Power Forecasting using continual learning to adapt to nonstationary conditions in real-world energy markets, systematically evaluating six approaches under realistic operational constraints.
AI.motivation: Power forecasting models in real-world energy markets face nonstationary conditions with evolving data distributions (weather variability, infrastructure upgrades, changing consumption), plus strict operational constraints like limited historical data availability and requirements for uninterrupted long-term service.
AI.method: Proposes the Continuous Power Forecasting paradigm using continual learning. Systematically investigates six representative continual learning approaches from three methodological categories, evaluated under different realistic assumptions about data accessibility and update policies using an adaptive continual learning framework for regression.
AI.result: Experimental validation on real-world power datasets shows that continual learning enables forecasting models to self-adapt to distributional drift, accumulate knowledge over time, and mitigate catastrophic forgetting without needing large-scale historical data storage. Provides practical insights into stability and adaptation behaviors of different approaches under realistic constraints.
AI.conclusion: Continual learning can be pragmatically integrated into industrial power forecasting pipelines, offering a scalable and sustainable solution for long-term deployment in dynamic environments, addressing the challenges of nonstationarity and operational constraints in real-world energy markets.

title: What Actually Works for Spacecraft Fault-Tolerant Control: An Honest Settled-Gate Benchmark of Learned and Classical Methods
summary: Recent learned fault-tolerant-control (FTC) work reports high success on spacecraft actuator faults, but often in simulation, on narrow fault sets, and with transient metrics that a trajectory need only touch once. We ask what recovers spacecraft pointing when success means holding it on faults never seen in training. We answer with a benchmark built around a settled gate, pointing held within 0.2 deg over a dwell window and scored on the true state, train/test splits disjoint in inertia, gain, sign pattern, and bias, Wilson intervals over n=500 episodes per cell, and one-command reproduction on a 6-DOF Basilisk testbed. Across classical, adaptive, learned end-to-end, and structured controllers, three findings stand out. Fault-unaware PD/PID and from-scratch end-to-end RL score 0%, so learning capacity alone is not the lever. Classical adaptive laws resolve sign faults but handle gain poorly at 55.2%, and a literature-faithful Nussbaum-gain law reaches 45.2% and 3.2%. A structured estimate-then-control design, with a learned recurrent module that infers actuator gain online and feeds an analytic law, wins on sign and gain faults at 97.8% and 94.4%, approaching the privileged oracle while unstructured methods remain at zero. The hard wall is constant additive bias, which is 0% for every controller including the privileged gain oracle, because an integral-free law cannot null a constant disturbance. We close it with a disturbance observer that recovers bias from the dynamics and is self-correcting for gain-estimate error. Composed with the gain estimate, it recovers 59.4% of held-out bias faults with no sign/gain regression, moving that class off zero. We classify sensor-fault regimes similarly, show that sensor bias is unobservable from the corrupted measurement alone and therefore requires fusion rather than an observer, and release the benchmark so the gate is shared.

AI.tldr: A benchmark for spacecraft fault-tolerant control that evaluates recovery from unseen actuator and sensor faults, finding that structured learned modules combined with analytic laws outperform both classical methods and end-to-end RL, with bias faults requiring disturbance observers and sensor faults requiring measurement fusion.
AI.motivation: Current learned fault-tolerant control methods for spacecraft often have limitations: they're evaluated in simulation only, test on narrow fault sets, and use transient metrics rather than sustained performance. The paper addresses what actually recovers spacecraft pointing when success requires holding it against faults never seen during training.
AI.method: They created a rigorous benchmark with: 1) A settled gate metric requiring pointing held within 0.2 degrees over a dwell window, 2) Strict train/test splits disjoint in inertia, gain, sign pattern, and bias, 3) Statistical evaluation with Wilson intervals over n=500 episodes per cell, 4) One-command reproduction on a 6-DOF Basilisk testbed. They tested classical controllers (PD/PID), adaptive laws, end-to-end RL, and structured controllers with learned modules.
AI.result: Key findings: 1) Fault-unaware PD/PID and end-to-end RL score 0% - learning capacity alone isn't sufficient, 2) Classical adaptive laws handle sign faults but only achieve 55.2% on gain faults, 3) Structured estimate-then-control design with learned recurrent modules for online gain inference achieves 97.8% on sign faults and 94.4% on gain faults, 4) Constant bias faults remain at 0% for all controllers until a disturbance observer is added, recovering 59.4% of bias faults, 5) Sensor bias is unobservable from corrupted measurements alone and requires measurement fusion.
AI.conclusion: Structured approaches combining learned modules for online inference with analytic control laws outperform both classical methods and unstructured learned controllers for spacecraft fault recovery. Different fault types require specific architectural solutions: gain/sign faults need online estimation, bias faults need disturbance observers, and sensor faults require measurement fusion rather than observers alone. The benchmark provides a rigorous evaluation framework for fault-tolerant control.

title: Convex--Concave Quadratic Spectral Filtering for Graph Neural Networks
summary: Spectral graph neural networks (GNNs) interpret message passing as frequency-selective filtering. While low-order spectral filters are efficient, their limited selectivity often leads to weak attenuation outside the passband, whereas high-order alternatives introduce optimization challenges. We propose DCQ-GNN, a spectral GNN based on a compact bank of adaptive convex--concave quadratic filters. By restricting the filter order to two while explicitly exploiting complementary curvature, DCQ-GNN improves spectral selectivity as quantified by Dirichlet energy and entropy measures without resorting to high-order polynomial expansions. The model fuses filter outputs through a node-adaptive gating mechanism to enable node-wise structure-aware spectral selection. We provide a formal spectral analysis grounded in Dirichlet energy attenuation, von Neumann entropy, and curvature polarity, and derive explicit characterizations of filter behavior across varying levels of homophily and structural perturbations. Extensive benchmarks on 10 datasets show that DCQ-GNN ties for the top average rank (3.0) on heterophilic graphs and obtains the second-best rank (4.2) on homophilic graphs, remaining competitive with representative high-order polynomial spectral filters. Furthermore, under strong structural perturbations, DCQ-GNN exhibits substantially smaller performance degradation compared to both first-order and high-order baselines. These results demonstrate that curvature-aware quadratic banks provide a robust and efficient alternative to high-order spectral models while preserving optimization stability and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: DCQ-GNN introduces a spectral GNN using compact convex-concave quadratic filters instead of high-order polynomials to achieve better spectral selectivity while maintaining optimization stability.
AI.motivation: Existing spectral GNNs face a trade-off: low-order filters lack spectral selectivity (weak attenuation outside passband) while high-order filters introduce optimization challenges and computational inefficiency.
AI.method: Proposes DCQ-GNN using a bank of adaptive convex-concave quadratic filters (order 2) with complementary curvature. Uses node-adaptive gating for structure-aware spectral selection. Formally analyzes spectral properties using Dirichlet energy, von Neumann entropy, and curvature polarity.
AI.result: Achieves top average rank (3.0) on heterophilic graphs and second-best rank (4.2) on homophilic graphs across 10 datasets. Shows substantially smaller performance degradation under structural perturbations compared to both low-order and high-order baselines.
AI.conclusion: Curvature-aware quadratic filters provide a robust, efficient alternative to high-order spectral models while preserving optimization stability and computational efficiency, achieving competitive performance across diverse graph scenarios.

title: Offline Multi-agent Continual Cooperation via Skill Partition and Reuse
summary: Extracting skills from multi-agent offline dataset improves learning efficiency via sharing task-invariant coordination skills among tasks. In settings where tasks occur sequentially and the space of skills grows exponentially, existing approaches that rely on heuristically designed and fixed-sized skill libraries struggle to resolve the problem of distributional shift and interference, facing catastrophic forgetting and plasticity loss. To address this problem and endow agents with the ability to continually discover and reuse coordination skills in open-environment, we propose COMAD, a principled framework for Continual Offline Multi-agent Skill Discovery via Skill Partition and Reuse. We first discover skills from mixed multi-agent behavior data with an auto-encoder to transform coordination knowledge into reusable coordination skills. Then we construct a skill-augmented policy learning objective with multi-head architectures, explicitly guiding the advantage function with reusable skills identified via a density-based reusability estimator. Theoretical analysis shows our method approximates the optimum of a continual skill discovery problem. Empirical results across diverse MARL benchmarks show that COMAD continually expands its skill library to mitigate interference, achieving superior forward and backward transfer for task streams compared to multiple baselines.

AI.tldr: COMAD is a continual offline multi-agent skill discovery framework that learns to partition and reuse coordination skills from multi-agent datasets to improve learning efficiency across sequential tasks, mitigating distributional shift and interference problems.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent skill discovery approaches struggle with distributional shift and interference when tasks occur sequentially, facing catastrophic forgetting and plasticity loss as the skill space grows exponentially. They rely on heuristically designed, fixed-sized skill libraries that can't adapt to open environments.
AI.method: COMAD discovers skills from mixed multi-agent behavior data using an auto-encoder to transform coordination knowledge into reusable skills. It constructs a skill-augmented policy learning objective with multi-head architectures, explicitly guiding the advantage function with skills identified via a density-based reusability estimator for skill partition and reuse.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows COMAD approximates the optimum of continual skill discovery. Empirical results across diverse MARL benchmarks demonstrate that COMAD continually expands its skill library to mitigate interference, achieving superior forward and backward transfer for task streams compared to multiple baselines.
AI.conclusion: COMAD provides a principled framework for continual offline multi-agent skill discovery that enables agents to continually discover and reuse coordination skills in open environments, effectively addressing distributional shift, interference, and plasticity loss problems in sequential task settings.

title: Swarm-Inspired Generation of Collective Behaviors in Graph Dynamical Systems
summary: Collective behavior arises when locally interacting units produce coordinated global organization, from synchronization in dynamical systems to task-relevant information flow on graphs. The central challenge is not only to explain how collective behavior emerges, but to design local interaction rules that can produce desired global organization and generalize across graphs, dynamics and tasks.To address this challenge, we introduce the Swarm-Inspired Emergent Synchronizer (SIES), a graph-dynamical framework that learns generalizable local-interaction laws for controllable collective organization. Each node is an agent-like dynamical unit with a state and task cue, and signed source-target-conditioned attention acts as an adaptive coupling term inside an explicit evolution model. Therefore, SIES combines an explicit dynamical engine with local agent intelligence, similar to biological swarms. For synchronization control, SIES learns a generalizable coupling operator that produces prescribed synchronization patterns for CDSs across untrained network scales, target phase relations, and intrinsic node dynamics without retraining. The learned operator also reaches gait-related modes faster than three oscillator baselines and generalizes synchronization-driven locomotion to simulated multi-legged robots of different scales and a physical hexapod after leg disablement. For graph representation learning, SIES applies the same signed interaction principle to message passing and achieves the highest performance among the compared methods on heterophilous node-classification benchmarks. Together, these results position SIES as a generalizable and learnable graph-dynamical interaction framework with promise for synchronization control, adaptive robot coordination, and heterophilous graph representation learning.

AI.tldr: SIES is a graph-dynamical framework that learns generalizable local interaction rules for collective behavior, combining explicit dynamics with agent intelligence to achieve synchronization control and graph representation learning.
AI.motivation: The main challenge is designing local interaction rules that can produce desired global organization and generalize across different graphs, dynamics, and tasks, moving beyond just explaining how collective behavior emerges.
AI.method: SIES (Swarm-Inspired Emergent Synchronizer) uses signed source-target-conditioned attention as an adaptive coupling term within an explicit evolution model, combining an explicit dynamical engine with local agent intelligence similar to biological swarms.
AI.result: For synchronization: SIES learns generalizable coupling operators that produce prescribed patterns across untrained network scales, phase relations, and node dynamics without retraining, achieving faster convergence than oscillator baselines and enabling robot locomotion. For graph learning: SIES achieves state-of-the-art performance on heterophilous node classification benchmarks using the same signed interaction principle.
AI.conclusion: SIES serves as a generalizable and learnable graph-dynamical interaction framework with promising applications in synchronization control, adaptive robot coordination, and heterophilous graph representation learning.

title: Long-Term Simulation Exposes Cognitive-Developmental Risks in AI Companions
summary: AI companions powered by large language models increasingly interact with cognition-developing users, including children and adolescents, creating risks that may accumulate over time. Existing safety evaluations largely rely on single-turn or short-session tests, which cannot capture risks that emerge only through prolonged interaction. To address this gap, we propose TSJ (Theater-Stage-Judge), a longitudinal framework combining persona-driven user simulation, dynamic psychological-state updating and retrospective evaluation. We evaluate six mainstream models across four developmental stages, twenty-four risk dimensions and three psychological-vulnerability personas, covering 12,960 simulated person-day interactions. TSJ shows that short-horizon testing systematically underestimates developmental risks, for which TSJ yields a stable risk estimate only after 140 turns within prolonged simulated relationships. Applying TSJ further identifies early childhood and emerging adulthood as the most vulnerable stages, with cognitive trust and emotional dependency as the weakest domains. TSJ provides a scalable methodology for longitudinal cognitive developmental risk evaluation in AI companion systems.

AI.tldr: TSJ is a longitudinal framework for evaluating AI companion risks to developing users through simulated long-term interactions, showing short-term tests underestimate developmental risks.
AI.motivation: Current AI companion safety evaluations use single-turn or short-session tests, which fail to capture risks that emerge only through prolonged interactions with cognition-developing users (children and adolescents). These risks can accumulate over time, creating potential developmental hazards.
AI.method: The TSJ (Theater-Stage-Judge) framework combines three components: 1) persona-driven user simulation, 2) dynamic psychological-state updating during interactions, and 3) retrospective evaluation. The system simulates 12,960 person-day interactions across six AI models, four developmental stages, twenty-four risk dimensions, and three psychological-vulnerability personas.
AI.result: Short-horizon testing systematically underestimates developmental risks - stable risk estimates only emerge after 140 turns within prolonged simulated relationships. Early childhood and emerging adulthood are the most vulnerable developmental stages. Cognitive trust and emotional dependency are the weakest domains. The framework reveals that risk patterns differ significantly between short-term and long-term interactions.
AI.conclusion: TSJ provides a scalable methodology for longitudinal cognitive developmental risk evaluation in AI companion systems, demonstrating that comprehensive safety assessment requires capturing emergent risks through prolonged interaction simulations rather than relying solely on short-term testing.

title: Reliable Conformal Prediction for Ordinal Classification Using the Ranked Probability Score
summary: Ordinal classification (OC) arises in high-stakes domains such as medicine and finance, where uncertainty quantification must account for the severity of ordinal errors. Conformal prediction (CP) provides distribution-free prediction sets with marginal coverage guarantees; however, its practical effectiveness depends critically on the choice of nonconformity function. We introduce a CP method for ordinal classification based on the ranked probability score (RPS), a proper scoring rule defined over cumulative predictive distributions. Although it reflects ordinal risk quite naturally, it has largely been neglected in conformal ordinal prediction (COP). When used as a measure of nonconformity, RPS yields median-centered contiguous prediction sets by construction. The method is model-agnostic, supports both assessed and grouped ordered categorical outcomes, and permits efficient implementation compared to greedy interval selection procedures. Across multiple ordinal image and tabular datasets, RPS-based CP produces contiguous prediction sets and strikes a favorable balance between prediction set width and the magnitude of ordinal miscoverage relative to existing CP methods.

AI.tldr: A conformal prediction method for ordinal classification using the Ranked Probability Score (RPS) as a nonconformity function, producing contiguous prediction sets that balance width and ordinal error severity.
AI.motivation: Ordinal classification in high-stakes domains like medicine and finance requires uncertainty quantification that accounts for the severity of ordinal errors. While conformal prediction provides coverage guarantees, practical effectiveness depends on nonconformity functions, and existing methods don't properly address ordinal risk.
AI.method: Introduces a CP method for ordinal classification using the Ranked Probability Score (RPS) as a nonconformity function. RPS is a proper scoring rule defined over cumulative predictive distributions. The method is model-agnostic, supports both assessed and grouped ordered categorical outcomes, and enables efficient implementation compared to greedy interval selection.
AI.result: RPS-based CP produces median-centered contiguous prediction sets by construction. Across multiple ordinal image and tabular datasets, it strikes a favorable balance between prediction set width and the magnitude of ordinal miscoverage compared to existing CP methods.
AI.conclusion: The RPS-based conformal prediction method effectively addresses ordinal classification uncertainty by naturally reflecting ordinal risk through the RPS scoring rule, producing practical and efficient prediction sets with proper coverage guarantees for high-stakes applications.

title: BrainAgent: A Large Language Model-Driven Multi-Agent Framework for Autonomous Brain Signal Understanding
summary: Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs) and brain signal understanding are pivotal for clinical health and next-generation interactions. Despite this significance, its widespread adoption in real-world scenarios remains restricted, primarily because current analytical paradigms lack sufficient agentic intelligence. First, existing methodologies impose prohibitive technical barriers, requiring extensive specialized expertise. Second, they remain inherently static and task-specific, failing to execute the complex, long-horizon workflows essential for real-world deployment. To accelerate the democratization of brain signal understanding, we draw inspiration from Large Language Models (LLMs) to introduce BrainAgent, an LLM-driven multi-agent framework designed to ground abstract natural language intent into rigorous, executable, and end-to-end processing pipelines. BrainAgent employs a hierarchical architecture where a central supervisor orchestrates specialized sub-agents for adaptive task decomposition and execution. Furthermore, we establish a comprehensive, systematic benchmark for evaluating agentic systems in brain signal analysis. Empirical results demonstrate that BrainAgent effectively automates complex workflows with superior reliability, marking a paradigm shift toward democratized brain signal understanding.

AI.tldr: BrainAgent is an LLM-driven multi-agent framework that automates complex brain signal analysis workflows, addressing technical barriers and task specificity limitations of current BCI systems.
AI.motivation: Current Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) systems face limited real-world adoption due to prohibitive technical barriers requiring specialized expertise, and their static, task-specific nature that fails to execute complex, long-horizon workflows needed for practical deployment.
AI.method: Introduces BrainAgent, an LLM-driven multi-agent framework with hierarchical architecture: a central supervisor orchestrates specialized sub-agents to ground abstract natural language intent into rigorous, executable, end-to-end processing pipelines for brain signal understanding.
AI.result: BrainAgent effectively automates complex workflows with superior reliability, as demonstrated through empirical results and a comprehensive systematic benchmark established for evaluating agentic systems in brain signal analysis.
AI.conclusion: BrainAgent represents a paradigm shift toward democratized brain signal understanding by leveraging LLMs to overcome current limitations in BCI technology, potentially accelerating widespread adoption in real-world clinical and interactive applications.

title: Enhancing Clinician Decision-Making via Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Expert Fusion for Stroke Rehabilitation
summary: Tailoring stroke rehabilitation requires assessing how movements are organized, not merely if they succeed. Currently, this assessment is a rate-limiting bottleneck. Instruments like the Action Research Arm Test (ARAT) compress rich behavioral observations into single ordinal endpoints, discarding the movement-quality details that distinguish recovery from compensation. Automated alternatives typically chase accuracy on noisy, single-observer labels to output opaque scores - a technology-centric approach that rarely reaches clinical practice. To address this, we present xAARA: an engine designed to augment rather than replace clinical judgment. From multi-view video, xAARA returns ARAT assessments with calibrated uncertainty and explanations across task, movement-phase, and movement-quality levels. Treating clinical scoring as an ill-posed inference problem, xAARA composes 692 calibrated multimodal models via a Dynamic Bayesian Network with entropy-based gating. It qualifies results against clinical validity rules and defers low-confidence cases. In 105 stroke survivors (788 exercises), xAARA achieved 94.2% task accuracy (Cohen's kappa=0.934) and 81.3% movement-phase accuracy (kappa=0.727), reducing predictive uncertainty by 96.1% compared to single-clinician scoring. For subjective cases, it matched at least one rater 100% of the time and never returned out-of-range scores. Four independent clinicians validated the assessments and indicated willingness to adopt the system. We argue that principled uncertainty quantification and clinician-aligned explainability are the critical bridges moving automated assessment from technical demonstration to a deployable clinical tool.

AI.tldr: xAARA is an automated assessment system for stroke rehabilitation that uses multi-view video analysis and calibrated uncertainty quantification to complement clinical judgment rather than replace it, achieving high accuracy while providing explainable results.
AI.motivation: Current stroke rehabilitation assessment methods like ARAT compress rich behavioral data into single ordinal scores, losing movement-quality details. Automated alternatives focus on chasing accuracy with opaque scoring and rarely reach clinical practice. There's a need for systems that augment rather than replace clinical judgment with uncertainty quantification and explainability.
AI.method: xAARA treats clinical scoring as an ill-posed inference problem and uses a Dynamic Bayesian Network with entropy-based gating to compose 692 calibrated multimodal models. It analyzes multi-view video to provide ARAT assessments with calibrated uncertainty and explanations at task, movement-phase, and movement-quality levels, qualifying results against clinical validity rules and deferring low-confidence cases.
AI.result: In 105 stroke survivors (788 exercises), xAARA achieved 94.2% task accuracy (Cohen's kappa=0.934) and 81.3% movement-phase accuracy (kappa=0.727), reducing predictive uncertainty by 96.1% compared to single-clinician scoring. For subjective cases, it matched at least one rater 100% of the time and never returned out-of-range scores. Four independent clinicians validated assessments and indicated willingness to adopt the system.
AI.conclusion: Principled uncertainty quantification and clinician-aligned explainability are critical bridges for moving automated assessment from technical demonstration to deployable clinical tools, as demonstrated by xAARA's success in augmenting rather than replacing clinical judgment in stroke rehabilitation assessment.

title: Quantization Inflates Reasoning: Token Inflation as a Hidden Cost of Low-Bit Reasoning Models
summary: Quantization is widely used to reduce the inference cost of large language models, but its effect on reasoning models is not fully captured by final-answer accuracy or per-token latency. We show that low-bit post-training quantization can introduce a hidden test-time compute cost: quantized reasoning models often generate longer chains of thought even when they still answer correctly. Across mathematical reasoning, code generation, scientific question answering, and agentic tool-use benchmarks, we find that INT4/INT3 quantization can preserve accuracy but increase reasoning-token usage, offsetting the expected per-token speedup. To measure this effect, we introduce the CoT Token Inflation Ratio, which compares reasoning length between quantized and full-precision models averaged across all evaluation benchmarks. We further show that token inflation is accompanied by behavioral changes in the reasoning trace, including more intermediate steps and greater semantic repetition. These changes translate into measurable end-to-end real-world serving penalties. Finally, we evaluate mitigation strategies and find that prompting and decoding-time sampling offer inconsistent accuracy-length trade-offs, while quantization-aware training shows more promise in reducing both accuracy degradation and token inflation. Our results suggest that reasoning-token usage should be reported alongside accuracy when evaluating quantized reasoning models.

AI.tldr: Quantized reasoning models often generate longer chains of thought even when answering correctly, offsetting expected inference speedups and introducing hidden compute costs.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation metrics (final-answer accuracy and per-token latency) don't fully capture quantization's effects on reasoning models. There's a hidden test-time compute cost where quantized models produce longer reasoning chains.
AI.method: Introduce the CoT Token Inflation Ratio to measure reasoning length differences between quantized and full-precision models. Evaluate across mathematical reasoning, code generation, scientific QA, and agentic tool-use benchmarks. Analyze behavioral changes in reasoning traces and evaluate mitigation strategies including prompting, decoding-time sampling, and quantization-aware training.
AI.result: INT4/INT3 quantization preserves accuracy but significantly increases reasoning-token usage, offsetting per-token speedups. Quantized models show more intermediate steps and semantic repetition. Prompting/decoding strategies offer inconsistent trade-offs, while quantization-aware training better reduces both accuracy loss and token inflation.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-token usage should be reported alongside accuracy when evaluating quantized reasoning models. The hidden compute cost from longer reasoning chains can undermine expected inference speedups, making holistic evaluation crucial.

title: Towards Scalable Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning with Large Decision Models
summary:
Recent progress in large-scale sequence modeling has shown that a single model can learn useful representations across highly diverse data distributions. Inspired by these advances, we investigate whether a unified transformer policy can be trained across large collections of heterogeneous reinforcement learning environments.
  We introduce LDM-v0, a Large Decision Model trained offline on trajectories collected from thousands of environments spanning multiple domains and modalities. LDM-v0 is a multi-task, multi-modal transformer policy conditioned on histories of observations, actions, rewards, and termination signals, and trained through supervised next-action prediction over offline trajectories. We describe the environment infrastructure, automated data generation pipeline, model architecture, and training methodology used to build LDM-v0, and evaluate its performance across diverse environments. We show that a single pretrained model matches the performance of independently trained task-specific reference policies on approximately 1,000 environments including robotics, autonomous driving, inventory management, cybersecurity, trading, and video games. These results demonstrate the feasibility of large-scale offline pretraining across heterogeneous reinforcement learning environments using a single transformer policy.

AI.tldr: A unified transformer policy (LDM-v0) trained offline on thousands of diverse RL environments matches task-specific policy performance across multiple domains including robotics, driving, and games.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates whether a single unified transformer policy can be trained across large collections of heterogeneous reinforcement learning environments, inspired by recent advances in large-scale sequence modeling showing that single models can learn useful representations across diverse data distributions.
AI.method: Introduces LDM-v0, a multi-task, multi-modal transformer policy conditioned on histories of observations, actions, rewards, and termination signals, trained through supervised next-action prediction over offline trajectories. The approach includes environment infrastructure, automated data generation pipeline, model architecture, and training methodology.
AI.result: A single pretrained model matches the performance of independently trained task-specific reference policies on approximately 1,000 environments spanning robotics, autonomous driving, inventory management, cybersecurity, trading, and video games.
AI.conclusion: The results demonstrate the feasibility of large-scale offline pretraining across heterogeneous reinforcement learning environments using a single transformer policy, suggesting that unified models can effectively handle diverse RL tasks.

title: Cliff Tokens: Identifying Single-Token Failure Triggers in LLM Mathematical Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) reach high accuracy in mathematical reasoning, but individual traces on the same problem diverge; some arrive at the correct answer while others fail. Prior work analyzes failure at the step, chunk, or sentence level, or at tokens where failure has already occurred. Neither identifies the precise token that triggers the shift toward failure. We introduce the cliff token, a token where the token-wise potential drops significantly under an adaptive threshold that scales with the local token-wise potential, based on a one-sided two-proportion z-test. Across seven models and three mathematical reasoning benchmarks (GSM1K, MATH500, AIME 2025), cliff tokens act as failure triggers; deleting the first cliff token and resampling recovers pass@64 to 1.0, while keeping it limits recovery to between 0.71 and 1.00. We further introduce a cliff taxonomy of deterministic, uncertain, and sampled-off cliffs, defined by greedy choice and token entropy. Each type has distinct probabilistic characteristics, and the taxonomy generalizes across model scales. Finally, we validate the taxonomy via single-token preference optimization at cliff positions (Cliff-DPO). Trained on GSM8K, Cliff-DPO improves accuracy across benchmarks by up to +6.6. Optimizing at uncertain and sampled-off cliffs improves reasoning, while deterministic cliffs do not.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "cliff tokens" - specific tokens where mathematical reasoning failures are triggered in LLMs. Identifying and deleting these tokens enables recovery of correct answers, and a taxonomy of cliff types guides effective preference optimization for improved reasoning.
AI.motivation: While LLMs achieve high accuracy in mathematical reasoning, their traces on the same problem diverge with some succeeding and others failing. Existing failure analysis methods don't identify the precise token that triggers the shift from success to failure, leaving a gap in understanding and fixing reasoning breakdowns.
AI.method: Introduced the "cliff token" concept using an adaptive threshold based on token-wise potential drop, detected via one-sided two-proportion z-test. Developed a taxonomy of three cliff types: deterministic (greedy choice), uncertain (high entropy), and sampled-off (non-greedy). Validated with Cliff-DPO - single-token preference optimization at cliff positions.
AI.result: Cliff tokens act as failure triggers across 7 models and 3 benchmarks (GSM1K, MATH500, AIME 2025). Deleting first cliff token recovers pass@64 to 1.0 vs 0.71-1.00 with it kept. Cliff taxonomy generalizes across model scales. Cliff-DPO improves accuracy up to +6.6; optimizing at uncertain/sampled-off cliffs helps while deterministic cliffs don't.
AI.conclusion: Cliff tokens are precise failure triggers in mathematical reasoning, and their taxonomy provides actionable insights for improving LLM reasoning through targeted interventions like preference optimization at specific cliff types, advancing failure analysis beyond traditional methods.

title: Evidence for feature-specific error correction in LLMs
summary: Understanding the features of large language models (LLMs) is a central goal of interpretability. LLMs are commonly assumed to use superposition to represent more features than they have dimensions. They may not only represent features in superposition but also perform computation in superposition. Theory predicts that computing in superposition requires error correction that privileges feature directions over generic ones, but this prediction has not been tested empirically. We propose an empirical test of error correction in LLMs based on activation perturbations. Perturbing residual-stream activations, we find that they are robust to small perturbations--forming activation plateaus consistent with error correction--but less robust along candidate feature directions ("pure" directions, constructed from contrastive prompt pairs) than along mixtures of two such directions, indicating that the pure directions are privileged. We quantify this privilegedness by modeling the perturbation effect as a function of the $L^p$-norm of its decomposition into feature components. For $p=2$ the response is a quadratic form with at most as many nonzero eigenvalues as the residual-stream dimension, which cannot privilege the many feature directions superposition requires. $p>2$ lifts this constraint and is consistent with feature-specific error correction. We find $p>2$ for contrastive, MELBO, and SAE-decoder directions, and $p\approx2$ for random and PCA directions (controls). These results replicate across Gemma-2-9B, Qwen3-1.7B, Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Aya-Expanse-8B, and Yi-1.5-9B. We further validate our method on a toy model of error correction with known ground-truth features, recovering $p>2$ for true feature directions, degrading toward $2$ as we rotate away from them.

AI.tldr: LLMs show evidence of computing in superposition with feature-specific error correction, demonstrated through activation perturbation experiments showing privileged feature directions.
AI.motivation: To empirically test the theoretical prediction that computing in superposition requires error correction that privileges feature directions over generic ones, which hasn't been tested before.
AI.method: Perturb residual-stream activations and analyze robustness patterns. Quantify privilegedness using L^p-norm modeling of perturbation effects on feature component decompositions.
AI.result: Found p>2 for contrastive, MELBO, and SAE-decoder directions (privileged feature directions), and p≈2 for random and PCA directions (control directions), consistent with feature-specific error correction.
AI.conclusion: LLMs demonstrate empirical evidence of computing in superposition with feature-specific error correction, supporting theoretical predictions about how neural networks represent and compute with more features than dimensions.

title: Learning Dynamical Systems from Multiple Sparse Datasets: A Hierarchical Bayesian Modeling Approach
summary: Estimating parameters of dynamical systems from sparse, noisy, and irregularly sampled data is often severely ill-conditioned. When multiple related datasets are available, they provide additional information if the shared structure and variability are properly modeled. We propose a hierarchical Bayesian framework for probabilistic meta-learning in dynamical systems, modeling dataset-specific parameters as draws from a shared population distribution. A numerical ODE solver is embedded within gradient-based MCMC to enable efficient posterior inference of the shared population and dataset-specific parameter distribution. Experiments show improved predictive performance over unpooled methods, highlighting the potential for data-efficient system identification in settings with sparse data.

AI.tldr: Hierarchical Bayesian framework for meta-learning in dynamical systems improves parameter estimation from sparse, noisy data by sharing information across multiple related datasets.
AI.motivation: Parameter estimation for dynamical systems from sparse, noisy, irregular data is ill-conditioned. When multiple related datasets exist, they can provide valuable information if their shared structure and variability are properly modeled to improve data efficiency.
AI.method: Proposes a hierarchical Bayesian framework for probabilistic meta-learning where dataset-specific parameters are modeled as draws from a shared population distribution. Embeds numerical ODE solver within gradient-based MCMC for efficient posterior inference of shared population and dataset-specific parameter distributions.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate improved predictive performance over unpooled methods, showing the framework's effectiveness for data-efficient system identification in sparse data settings.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical Bayesian meta-learning approach enables more accurate and data-efficient parameter estimation for dynamical systems by leveraging shared structure across multiple datasets, particularly valuable in sparse data scenarios.

title: Reasonable Motion: A General ASP Foundation for Environment Constrained Movement Trajectory Computation
comment: Accepted at: LPNMR 2026 - 18th International Conference on Logic Programming and Non-monotonic Reasoning, 7 - 11 September 2026 - Klagenfurt, Austria
summary: We present a general answer set programming based hybrid quantitative-qualitative method for computing constrained branching trajectory modes for moving objects in real-world settings. The method performs constrained traversal of an environment graph, enumerating geometrically admissible motion behaviours as stable models, each constituting a distinct trajectory mode characterised by both domain-dependent and independent factors such as derived event sequence, map topology, and domain norms. The hybrid trajectory computation method is generally applicable across motion characteristics typically encountered in diverse dynamic domains with moving objects, e.g., autonomous driving. We demonstrate applicability and highlight how computed trajectories are traceable to their underlying stable model, thereby affording verifiable interpretability that purely learned approaches cannot provide. We also perform an empirical evaluation with Argoverse 2, a large-scale real-world autonomous driving benchmark representative of the class of dynamic domains within the scope of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: A hybrid ASP-based method for computing constrained branching trajectory modes with verifiable interpretability, demonstrated on autonomous driving benchmarks.
AI.motivation: There's a need for interpretable trajectory computation methods that combine quantitative and qualitative aspects, unlike purely learned approaches that lack traceability and verifiability.
AI.method: Uses Answer Set Programming (ASP) to perform constrained traversal of environment graphs, enumerating geometrically admissible motion behaviors as stable models. Each stable model constitutes a distinct trajectory mode characterized by both domain-dependent and independent factors.
AI.result: The method is generally applicable across motion characteristics in diverse dynamic domains, demonstrated empirically with Argoverse 2 autonomous driving benchmark. Computed trajectories are traceable to underlying stable models, providing verifiable interpretability.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid quantitative-qualitative ASP-based approach offers verifiable interpretability for trajectory computation that purely learned methods cannot provide, making it suitable for real-world applications like autonomous driving where traceability is crucial.

title: What Do Language Priors Contribute to Darcy-Flow Inversion? A Mechanistic Audit
summary: In ill-posed inverse problems, the recovered solution depends as much on the prior as on the data, yet much of the engineering knowledge that could serve as that prior is recorded qualitatively rather than in formal mathematical form. Here we test whether sentence embeddings can act as an inference-time interface for injecting geological descriptions into a learned Darcy-flow inverse solver. Across six synthetic geological classes and an exploratory transfer to a benchmark reservoir model (SPE10), we vary only the conditioning representation and find that text conditioning reduces reconstruction error by 81 % relative to a no-text counterfactual. Most of this gain comes from a categorical, class-level constraint whose value concentrates where the hydraulic head leaves the conductivity field underdetermined, while within-class geometric detail is secondary and pattern-dependent. Compared with a discrete class label, sentence embeddings add little dense-observation accuracy but improve training stability and enable paraphrase-based sensitivity analysis and open-vocabulary inputs. These results show that language priors can serve as an engineering-informatics interface for injecting geological knowledge into learned inverse solvers, while clarifying when they help and what signal they actually carry.

AI.tldr: Using text embeddings as inference-time priors for Darcy-flow inverse solvers reduces reconstruction error by 81% by injecting geological knowledge through language.
AI.motivation: In ill-posed inverse problems, engineering knowledge often exists as qualitative descriptions rather than formal mathematical priors. The authors want to test whether language can serve as an interface to inject geological knowledge into learned inverse solvers.
AI.method: Tested sentence embeddings as inference-time conditioning for a learned Darcy-flow inverse solver across six synthetic geological classes and transferred to SPE10 benchmark reservoir model. Varied only conditioning representations to isolate text's effect.
AI.result: Text conditioning reduced reconstruction error by 81% relative to no-text baseline. Most gain came from categorical, class-level constraints, especially where hydraulic head left conductivity field underdetermined. Within-class geometric detail was secondary. Sentence embeddings added little accuracy beyond discrete class labels but improved training stability and enabled paraphrase-based sensitivity analysis and open-vocabulary inputs.
AI.conclusion: Language priors can serve as an engineering-informatics interface for injecting geological knowledge into learned inverse solvers, clarifying when they help and what signal they actually carry (mainly categorical constraints rather than fine geometric details).

title: GUI agent: Guided Exploration of User-Sensitive Screens
summary: LLM agents are increasingly being used to automate tasks for users within an open GUI environment. They inevitably encounter screens containing user-sensitive information, for which takeover of task execution by the user is highly desirable or even necessary. State-of-the-art LLM-driven agents are usually fine-tuned to complete tasks regardless of the safety implications of their actions. This makes their real-world deployment difficult and adversely affects the reliability. Therefore, it is crucial to identify and categorize user-sensitive states and define user-sensitive queries. This dataset would be to engineers to recognize and request handover to the user in critical scenarios. This short paper develops an explorer agent that systematically explores the query space starting from one demonstrated task to identify queries that, if executed, would lead to user-sensitive states in a GUI environment.

AI.tldr: LLM agents in GUI environments need safety mechanisms to identify and avoid user-sensitive information, requiring handover to human users in critical scenarios.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-driven agents in GUI environments often act on user-sensitive information without safety considerations, making real-world deployment difficult and unreliable. There's a need to identify when agents should hand over control to users to prevent privacy/safety issues.
AI.method: Develops an explorer agent that systematically explores the query space starting from one demonstrated task to identify queries that would lead to user-sensitive states in GUI environments.
AI.result: The approach creates a method to identify and categorize user-sensitive states and define user-sensitive queries, providing a dataset for engineers to recognize when to request handover to users.
AI.conclusion: A systematic approach is needed to identify user-sensitive states in GUI environments where LLM agents operate, enabling safer deployment through proper handover mechanisms to human users.

title: Training Dynamics of Neural Software Defect Predictors under Coupled Data-Quality Issues
summary:
Context: Software defect prediction supports maintenance decisions such as testing prioritization, release-risk assessment, and quality monitoring. However, metric-based SDP datasets often contain coupled data-quality issues, especially class imbalance and class overlap. Prior work has mainly measured their impact through endpoint performance, while recent evidence suggests that such issues may also appear in neural training dynamics (gradients, weights, biases, error trajectories). However, these studies examine issues in isolation, leaving open how internal neural network training patterns manifest when data quality issues are coupled.
  Objective: We investigate how training-dynamics patterns from class imbalance, overlap, and their coupling can be characterized under interaction-aware conditions in deep learning-based SDP.
  Method: We conduct a controlled intervention study on class-level UBD datasets, training a fixed MLP under imbalance-only, overlap-only, and joint conditions across five seeds. Training dynamics are logged per epoch; fidelity is monitored via coupling ratios. Patterns are characterized using effect sizes, trajectories, sensitivity analyses, and rule-based classification.
  Expected contribution: The study will produce an interaction-aware empirical protocol and a candidate taxonomy of training-dynamics patterns for coupled data-quality issues in metric-based SDP.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates how coupled data-quality issues (class imbalance and overlap) manifest in neural network training dynamics for software defect prediction, moving beyond endpoint performance analysis.
AI.motivation: Software defect prediction (SDP) relies on metric-based datasets that often contain coupled data-quality issues like class imbalance and overlap. While prior work mainly measured their impact through final performance metrics, recent evidence suggests these issues also appear in neural training dynamics, but studies have examined them in isolation rather than exploring their coupled effects.
AI.method: A controlled intervention study on class-level unbalanced datasets, training a fixed MLP under three conditions: imbalance-only, overlap-only, and joint coupling. Training dynamics are logged per epoch with fidelity monitoring via coupling ratios. Patterns are characterized using effect sizes, trajectories, sensitivity analyses, and rule-based classification.
AI.result: The study is expected to produce both an interaction-aware empirical protocol and a candidate taxonomy of training-dynamics patterns for coupled data-quality issues in metric-based SDP.
AI.conclusion: The research aims to provide deeper insights into how coupled data-quality issues manifest in neural network training dynamics, moving beyond simple endpoint performance analysis to understand the internal patterns that emerge during training, which could inform better SDP model development and quality assessment.

title: Position Spaces and Graphs
summary: In this paper, we introduce position graphs, a graph-based reasoning framework based on the formalization of position spaces. This framework utilizes two strict partial orders, representing horizontal and vertical alignment and precedence, to model the relative positions of discrete tokens. Unlike general qualitative spatial calculi, position graphs are constrained by a chain condition and compatibility requirements that focus on rows and columns. We provide a comprehensive theoretical analysis of this representation, beginning with a characterization of graph consistency. Conditions to ensure the consistency of position graphs are established. Furthermore, we investigate the computational complexity of structural pattern discovery, modeled as the induced subgraph isomorphism problem. We demonstrate that this problem remains NP-complete even within the restricted class of position graphs. While initially motivated by document processing, this work focuses on the underlying mathematical properties and algebraic consistency of position-based constraints, providing a formal logical layer that is independent of specific data extraction techniques.

AI.tldr: Position graphs: a graph-based reasoning framework using two strict partial orders (horizontal/vertical) to model discrete token positions, with theoretical analysis showing NP-completeness of induced subgraph isomorphism despite constraints.
AI.motivation: To develop a formal mathematical framework for modeling relative positions of discrete tokens, initially motivated by document processing but focusing on underlying mathematical properties independent of specific applications.
AI.method: Introduce position graphs based on position space formalization with two strict partial orders representing horizontal/vertical alignment/precedence, constrained by chain conditions and compatibility requirements focusing on rows and columns.
AI.result: Provide comprehensive theoretical analysis including characterization of graph consistency, establish conditions for ensuring consistency, and prove that induced subgraph isomorphism problem remains NP-complete even within restricted position graphs class.
AI.conclusion: Position graphs offer a formal logical layer for position-based constraints with rigorous mathematical foundations, demonstrating computational complexity limitations while providing a general framework applicable beyond initial document processing motivation.

title: Frequency Domain Reservoir Computing
summary: While the quadratic sequence-length bottleneck of transformers has fueled a resurgence in recurrent models, effectively capturing complex dynamics requires architectures that balance efficient training with highly expressive latent states. Echo State Networks (ESNs) offer a compelling approach by utilizing fixed recurrent weights to circumvent backpropagation through time, enabling a closed-form training solution. However, achieving the expressivity needed for complex tasks demands large reservoirs, exposing an $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ state-update bottleneck that prevents ESNs from matching the scale of contemporary recurrent models. To address this limitation, we introduce Frequency Domain Reservoir Computing (FRESCO), an ESN architecture operating entirely in the frequency domain while avoiding domain-shift overheads to achieve $\mathcal{O}(N)$ complexity for dense, non-linear recurrent updates. By employing a novel dimensional zero-padding input embedding, a packed \FDh readout, and a natively applied frequency-domain non-linearity, FRESCO drastically reduces computational costs and energy consumption of training and inference. Furthermore, FRESCO matches the state-of-the-art predictive performance on memory benchmarks, sequential classification, and multivariate long-horizon forecasting, offering a scalable path forward for dense recurrent architectures.

AI.tldr: FRESCO is a new Echo State Network architecture that operates in the frequency domain to achieve linear complexity (O(N)) instead of quadratic (O(N²)), enabling efficient training and inference while maintaining state-of-the-art performance on various sequential tasks.
AI.motivation: While Echo State Networks offer efficient training with fixed recurrent weights, they suffer from an O(N²) computational bottleneck in state updates, preventing them from scaling to match modern recurrent models despite their theoretical advantages in avoiding backpropagation through time.
AI.method: FRESCO operates entirely in the frequency domain using: 1) dimensional zero-padding input embedding, 2) packed frequency-domain readout, and 3) natively applied frequency-domain non-linearity to achieve O(N) complexity for dense non-linear recurrent updates without domain-shift overheads.
AI.result: FRESCO drastically reduces computational costs and energy consumption for both training and inference while matching state-of-the-art predictive performance on memory benchmarks, sequential classification tasks, and multivariate long-horizon forecasting problems.
AI.conclusion: FRESCO provides a scalable path forward for dense recurrent architectures by eliminating the quadratic bottleneck of traditional ESNs while maintaining their training efficiency advantages, potentially enabling recurrent models to compete more effectively with contemporary large-scale sequence models.

title: Fuzzy Quantification over OWL Ontologies and Knowledge Graphs
summary: This paper presents a versatile framework for evaluating fuzzy quantification queries over both standard and fuzzy ontologies as well as knowledge graphs. The primary objective is the retrieval of individuals that satisfy queries articulated via Type I or Type II fuzzy quantified expressions. A key advantage of the proposed approach is its inherent adaptability: it remains entirely agnostic to the quantifier type, the underlying evaluation method, and the specific data source of the ontology (i.e., OWL ontologies or RDFS knowledge graphs). Furthermore, we present Q2S2, a publicly accessible implementation of this system developed to support future research.

AI.tldr: A framework for evaluating fuzzy quantification queries over ontologies and knowledge graphs, supporting Type I/II fuzzy expressions, with adaptable architecture and an open-source implementation called Q2S2.
AI.motivation: The need for versatile evaluation of fuzzy quantification queries across different types of ontologies (standard/fuzzy) and knowledge graphs, supporting various fuzzy quantified expressions while being independent of specific implementation details.
AI.method: A flexible framework that is agnostic to quantifier type, evaluation method, and data source (OWL ontologies or RDFS knowledge graphs). The system retrieves individuals satisfying Type I or Type II fuzzy quantified queries.
AI.result: Q2S2 - a publicly accessible implementation of the framework developed to support future research, demonstrating the framework's practical applicability.
