AI.method: Hierarchical ensemble-reinforcement learning with two abstraction levels: low-level agents handle per-kernel configurations per block (bitwidth, pruning ratio, quantization type/granularity), while high-level agents coordinate global budget via ensemble voting using Fisher Information-based sensitivity estimates. Uses an iterative active learning loop with surrogate-guided RL optimization and post-compression fine-tuning, with MLP surrogate for reward shaping.
AI.result: Achieves 5.99-6.72× parameter-storage compression ratios on Vision Transformer and CNN benchmarks, with 3.83% accuracy gain in one setting and 0.55-5.62% accuracy drops in others, demonstrating practical effectiveness of hierarchical decomposition and sensitivity-aware guidance.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical policy decomposition and sensitivity-aware guidance are effective design choices for joint neural network compression, and the proposed HiReLC framework successfully automates the complex compression search while being architecture-agnostic and computationally efficient.

title: Stagnant Neuron: Towards Understanding the Plasticity Loss in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Value Factorization Methods
summary: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) value factorization methods can suffer from a loss of plasticity, gradually failing to adapt when transferring to new task instances. We trace this issue to stagnant neurons, units whose gradient updates become negligibly small relative to their weights, thereby hindering learning. While existing plasticity injection methods exist, they prove ineffective for such neurons. To address this, we propose Knowledge-retentive Neuron-level PlastIcity Focusing InjEction (KNIFE), a novel method that directly targets stagnant neurons. KNIFE replaces each stagnant neuron with a composite unit comprising three specialized components: a frozen knowledge neuron to preserve acquired knowledge, a re-initialized active neuron to restore learning capacity, and a compensation neuron to ensure the combined output matches the original, thus maintaining previous learned cooperation knowledge. Extensive experiments on SMACv2, predator-prey, and matrix games demonstrate that KNIFE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art plasticity injection methods.

AI.tldr: KNIFE is a novel MARL method that targets stagnant neurons by replacing them with composite units to restore plasticity while preserving learned knowledge, outperforming existing methods in various multi-agent environments.
AI.motivation: MARL value factorization methods suffer from loss of plasticity when transferring to new task instances, with stagnant neurons (units with negligible gradient updates relative to weights) hindering learning. Existing plasticity injection methods are ineffective for these neurons.
AI.method: Proposes Knowledge-retentive Neuron-level PlastIcity Focusing InjEction (KNIFE), which replaces each stagnant neuron with a composite unit consisting of three specialized components: 1) a frozen knowledge neuron to preserve acquired knowledge, 2) a re-initialized active neuron to restore learning capacity, and 3) a compensation neuron to ensure combined output matches the original, maintaining previous learned cooperation knowledge.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on SMACv2, predator-prey, and matrix games demonstrate that KNIFE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art plasticity injection methods.
AI.conclusion: KNIFE effectively addresses the loss of plasticity problem in MARL value factorization methods by directly targeting stagnant neurons with a specialized composite replacement strategy, enabling better adaptation to new task instances while preserving existing cooperation knowledge.

title: Natural Ungrokking: Asymmetric Control of Which Rules Survive Pretraining
comment: Foundations of Deep Generative Models (FoGen) Workshop at ICML 2026. 23 pages (5-page main text plus appendices), 5 figures. Code: https://github.com/lijuliana/Natural-Ungrokking
summary:
Midway through an ordinary pretraining run, a small language model learns the pronoun-gender rule: cued with a girl's name ("Sue cried because"), it resolves the next pronoun to she, generalizing to held-out probes (0.94 by step 925). By step 3,500 the same model scores near zero on the same probes, although the rule's evidence is still in the training data. We call this within-run reversal natural ungrokking: the corpus decides, with no trace in the loss curve, which learned rules a model keeps.
  Which rules survive is predictable from one corpus statistic: how often the training stream shows the rule winning. Across un-intervened runs (two corpora, three budgets, three seeds), support frequency decides a rule's fate; the data-to-parameter ratio only modulates how deeply a doomed rule falls. The same emerge-then-collapse dynamics appear in public Pythia checkpoints, collapse depth ordered by model scale as predicted. The forgetting is a displacement: a competing surface pattern out-competes the rule, and the log-probability margin between them crosses zero within 100 training steps of the behavioral collapse.
  Control over this fate is asymmetric: the same edit that destroys a rule on demand cannot restore it. Flipping support to counter-evidence in place kills the rule with monotone dose-response in two unrelated rules; but injecting support back, even to 450 times the level that naturally sustains it, buys no recovery. Every confirmatory threshold and prediction was pre-registered before the data it governed was read.

AI.tldr: Small language models learn pronoun-gender rules early in training but later forget them despite evidence still being in the data, with rule survival predictable from training stream frequency rather than loss metrics.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates why certain rules learned by language models during pretraining don't survive throughout training, even when the supporting evidence remains in the training data, and explores what determines which rules models retain versus forget.
AI.method: Researchers tracked pronoun-gender rule learning in small language models during pretraining, measuring generalization to held-out probes. They analyzed rule dynamics across different training budgets, corpora, and random seeds, examining public Pythia checkpoints. They conducted controlled experiments by editing training data to manipulate rule support frequency.
AI.result: Rules emerge early (0.94 accuracy by step 925) then collapse to near zero by step 3,500. Rule survival is determined solely by how often the training stream shows the rule "winning" (support frequency). Collapse occurs through displacement by competing surface patterns, with log-probability margin crossing zero within 100 steps. Data manipulations can destroy rules but not restore them, showing asymmetric control.
AI.conclusion: Natural ungrokking occurs when corpus statistics determine which learned rules models keep, with support frequency being the key predictor. Rules can be forgotten through displacement by competing patterns, and this forgetting is irreversible through simple data manipulation, suggesting deeper learning dynamics than surface statistics alone.

title: Lifelong In-Context Learning with Transformers Requires Parametric Forms of Attention
summary: Lifelong continual learning remains an obstacle on the path to human-like intelligence. Modern transformers show sparks of intelligence with in-context learning. The quadratic nature of attention, however, prohibits transformers from performing this process on arbitrarily long sequences. In this work, we argue that extending in-context learning to lifelong settings is a practical solution for continual learning in AI agents. In particular, we argue that \emph{parametric forms of attention} are needed to understand a lifetime of context with transformers on a fixed hardware budget. These attention mechanisms learn the relationship between keys and their associated values at test-time with parametric regression. Our generalization of parametric approaches (linear attention, state-space models, fast weight programmers, and test-time training layers) contrasts with nonparametric counterparts like softmax attention. They replace the ever-growing key-value cache with an online-trainable neural network, maintaining a constant memory footprint. We highlight how parametric attention currently fall short of lifelong learning due to limited memory capacity or costly online updates. To address these issues, we pose a set of open questions with novel insights to guide the field toward long-horizon agents.

AI.tldr: Parametric attention mechanisms (like linear attention and state-space models) are needed for transformers to perform lifelong continual learning by replacing ever-growing key-value caches with online-trainable networks, though current implementations still face limitations.
AI.motivation: Current transformers struggle with lifelong continual learning due to quadratic attention costs that prevent processing arbitrarily long sequences. In-context learning shows promise but needs to be extended to lifelong settings for practical AI agents.
AI.method: The paper argues for parametric attention mechanisms that learn key-value relationships at test-time through parametric regression, replacing the growing key-value cache with online-trainable neural networks to maintain constant memory footprint.
AI.result: The analysis shows current parametric approaches (linear attention, state-space models, fast weight programmers, test-time training layers) still fall short of true lifelong learning due to limited memory capacity or costly online updates.
AI.conclusion: The paper poses open questions and provides novel insights to guide the field toward developing parametric attention mechanisms capable of supporting long-horizon AI agents with true lifelong learning capabilities.

title: Model Forensics: Investigating Whether Concerning Behavior Reflects Misalignment
summary: A central goal of safety research is determining whether a model is misaligned. Prior work has largely focused on detecting concerning behavior. But behavior alone does not establish misalignment: a concerning action can arise from benign causes such as confusion. This motivates model forensics: investigating whether the action was driven by malign intent. In this paper, we propose a baseline protocol for model forensics consisting of two steps, iterated as needed. First, we read the chain of thought (CoT) to generate hypotheses about what drives model behavior. Second, we make edits to the prompt or environment to test these hypotheses. While the CoT is not always faithful, it is a rich source of unsupervised insight that can guide the collection of more rigorous evidence. To evaluate our protocol, we create a suite of six agentic environments where models exhibit concerning behavior, and apply it to each. We establish that Kimi K2 Thinking takes shortcuts due to a genuine disposition towards low-effort actions, by showing this hypothesis successfully predicts its behavior. Through counterfactual experiments, we show DeepSeek R1 deceives out of a desire to be consistent with a previous instance of itself. Our methods nonetheless leave significant room for refinement. For example, when we test whether Kimi K2 Thinking believes it is violating user intent, we find no evidence of such a belief, but without positive controls we cannot confirm our tests would detect it. Overall, we find our simple protocol provides a strong baseline that we hope future work will improve upon. More broadly, our work is a concrete step in developing the growing field of model forensics.

AI.tldr: A baseline protocol for model forensics that uses chain-of-thought analysis and hypothesis testing to investigate whether concerning model behavior stems from malign intent rather than benign causes like confusion.
AI.motivation: Traditional safety research focuses on detecting concerning behavior, but behavior alone doesn't establish misalignment - concerning actions can arise from benign causes like confusion. This motivates the need for model forensics to investigate whether actions are driven by malign intent.
AI.method: A two-step protocol: 1) Read chain-of-thought to generate hypotheses about what drives model behavior, 2) Make edits to prompts or environments to test these hypotheses. The protocol is applied to six agentic environments where models exhibit concerning behavior.
AI.result: Successfully established that Kimi K2 Thinking takes shortcuts due to genuine low-effort disposition, and DeepSeek R1 deceives to maintain consistency with previous instances. Found no evidence that Kimi K2 Thinking believes it's violating user intent, but noted limitations due to lack of positive controls.
AI.conclusion: The simple protocol provides a strong baseline for model forensics that future work should improve upon. The research represents a concrete step in developing the growing field of model forensics, though methods leave significant room for refinement.

title: Geometry-Anchored Transport Framework for Exemplar-Free Class-Incremental Learning
comment: Accepted to ECCV 2026. 17 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Code: https://github.com/HXuSz11/GATF_ECCV2026
summary: Exemplar-free class-incremental learning (EFCIL) requires stable decision boundaries within a shifting feature space. While maintaining class-conditional Gaussian statistics provides a principled classification strategy, these parametric summaries remain sensitive to anisotropic representation drift. Existing methods often transport these statistics across tasks using a decoupled, post-hoc paradigm: optimizing a backbone without explicit geometric constraints can distort the legacy manifold, limiting the precision of retroactive alignment. In this paper, we formulate feature transport as an endogenous training constraint rather than a separate post-task step, presenting the Geometry-Anchored Transport Framework. First, we derive an Analytic Geometric Anchor via Mahalanobis-aligned regression to mitigate macroscopic anisotropic drift. Second, we introduce a Topology-Aware Evolution objective that regularizes localized manifold degradation while calibrating a residual network against the analytic prior. By coupling manifold evolution with transport constraints during the primary training phase, our framework mitigates evaluation errors without requiring decoupled fine-tuning. Experiments across CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet, and ImageNet-100 demonstrate that the proposed framework consistently improves upon existing post-hoc alternatives under strict exemplar-free constraints.

AI.tldr: A framework for exemplar-free class-incremental learning that integrates feature transport constraints directly into training rather than using post-hoc alignment, improving stability in shifting feature spaces.
AI.motivation: Existing EFCIL methods suffer from anisotropic representation drift when using class-conditional Gaussian statistics, and post-hoc transport paradigms can distort legacy manifolds, limiting retroactive alignment precision.
AI.method: 1) Analytic Geometric Anchor via Mahalanobis-aligned regression to mitigate macroscopic anisotropic drift; 2) Topology-Aware Evolution objective to regularize localized manifold degradation while calibrating residual network against analytic prior.
AI.result: The framework consistently improves upon existing post-hoc alternatives across CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet, and ImageNet-100 under strict exemplar-free constraints.
AI.conclusion: Coupling manifold evolution with transport constraints during primary training phase mitigates evaluation errors without requiring decoupled fine-tuning, offering better stability for EFCIL.

title: Neglected Free Lunch from Post-training: Progress Advantage for LLM Agents
summary: Process reward models enable fine-grained, step-level evaluation of LLMs, yet building them for agentic settings remains prohibitively difficult: long-horizon interactions, irreversible actions, and stochastic environment feedback make both human annotation and Monte Carlo estimation infeasible at scale. In this work, we show that reinforcement learning (RL) post-training already provides the ingredients for effective step-level scoring, eliminating the need for dedicated reward model training altogether. Concretely, we derive an implicit advantage under a general stochastic Markov decision process, which we term progress advantage -- log-probability ratio between the RL-trained policy and its reference policy exactly recovers the optimal advantage function. This formulation makes the resulting signal annotation-free, domain-agnostic, and available as a byproduct of the standard RL post-training pipeline. We validate the effectiveness of the progress advantage across three different applications: test-time scaling, uncertainty quantification, and failure attribution on five benchmarks and four model families. Across all settings, it consistently outperforms confidence-based baselines and, despite requiring no task-specific training, surpasses dedicated trained reward models. We complement these results with deeper analyses on characteristics of progress advantage, offering practical guidance for adoption in real-world agentic systems.

AI.tldr: RL post-training creates implicit step-level reward signals without needing dedicated reward models, enabling effective evaluation of agentic LLMs across various applications.
AI.motivation: Building process reward models for agentic settings is extremely difficult due to long-horizon interactions, irreversible actions, and stochastic environment feedback. Both human annotation and Monte Carlo estimation are infeasible at scale for such settings.
AI.method: The authors derive an implicit advantage function called "progress advantage" from standard RL post-training. This is calculated as the log-probability ratio between the RL-trained policy and its reference policy, which theoretically recovers the optimal advantage function without additional training.
AI.result: Progress advantage consistently outperforms confidence-based baselines across three applications (test-time scaling, uncertainty quantification, failure attribution) on five benchmarks and four model families. Despite requiring no task-specific training, it surpasses dedicated trained reward models.
AI.conclusion: RL post-training already provides effective step-level scoring capabilities as a byproduct, eliminating the need for dedicated reward model training in agentic systems, making step-level evaluation feasible at scale.

title: On-Policy Self-Distillation with Sampled Demonstrations Reduces Output Diversity
summary: On-policy self-distillation achieves strong pass@1 accuracy by using a single model as both teacher and student, with the teacher conditioned on a correct demonstration to provide dense token-level feedback. We show that this could come at a hidden cost: rollout diversity decreases and pass@k curves flatten (i.e., generating more rollouts fails to improve accuracy). We trace this to compounding biases in the design of self-distillation with sampled demonstrations. The teacher scores each student rollout while conditioned on a sampled correct rollout, channeling its feedback through the model's own biases. We theoretically analyze the optimal self-distillation policy and show that it tilts the base distribution by a pointwise conditional mutual information score between the student's rollout and the correct rollout used as context. Unlike the ideal optimal on-policy reinforcement learning (RL), which preserves probability ratios among equally correct rollouts, self-distillation can amplify existing probability gaps, concentrating mass on already-dominant modes. On a controlled graph path-finding task and science question-answering benchmarks, self-distilled models match or exceed RL on average performance but exhibit substantially lower functional and semantic diversity, failing on out-of-distribution settings that require diverse strategies.

AI.tldr: On-policy self-distillation for code generation achieves strong pass@1 accuracy but reduces rollout diversity and fails to improve pass@k with more samples, due to compounding biases that concentrate probability mass on dominant modes instead of preserving diversity like optimal RL.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates a hidden cost of on-policy self-distillation: while it achieves strong pass@1 accuracy by using a single model as both teacher and student with correct demonstration conditioning, this approach may reduce rollout diversity and flatten pass@k curves, meaning generating more rollouts doesn't improve accuracy.
AI.method: The authors theoretically analyze the optimal self-distillation policy, showing it tilts the base distribution by a pointwise conditional mutual information score between student rollout and correct rollout context. They compare this to ideal optimal on-policy RL, conduct empirical evaluation on controlled graph path-finding tasks, and test on science question-answering benchmarks.
AI.result: Self-distilled models match or exceed RL on average performance but exhibit substantially lower functional and semantic diversity. The compounding biases cause the teacher to channel feedback through the model's own biases, amplifying existing probability gaps and concentrating mass on already-dominant modes rather than preserving probability ratios among equally correct rollouts.
AI.conclusion: On-policy self-distillation has a trade-off: while effective for pass@1 accuracy, it sacrifices diversity which is crucial for pass@k performance and out-of-distribution generalization. Unlike optimal RL which preserves diversity, self-distillation's compounding biases reduce rollout variety, making models fail on tasks requiring diverse strategies.

title: DFMU: Data-Frugal Machine Unlearning
summary: Machine unlearning is an emerging domain that ensures the safe removal of elements (includes concepts, attributes, entity and class) from the trained model along with least drop in model performance. The domain of machine unlearning brings its own indigenous challenges since the removal of pre-trained elements from model will always degrade the model performance on remaining elements. The existing methods basically rely on retraining for removal of elements from the pre-trained model, which is compute extensive. In this work, we propose a machine unlearning method which helps to reduce the computational requirement for faster retain-dataset accuracy convergence which also does not require extensive retraining of the pre-trained model. The proposed method, Data-Frugal Machine Unlearning (DFMU) requires only a single forward and backward pass for computing the importance score of various computational blocks of a model. The importance score computation is based on knowledge preserving pruning which helps to converge faster and requires far less data as compared to the existing methods. Experimentally, it achieves 40% more retain-accuracy with just 13% of data samples in comparison with SOTA method on various public datasets and also averages 88% faster processing time for forgetting a given class.

AI.tldr: DFMU: A data-efficient machine unlearning method that requires only a single forward/backward pass to compute importance scores, achieving faster convergence and reduced computational overhead compared to retraining-based approaches.
AI.motivation: Current machine unlearning methods rely heavily on retraining, which is computationally expensive and time-consuming. There's a need for more efficient approaches that minimize performance degradation while removing elements from trained models.
AI.method: Proposes Data-Frugal Machine Unlearning (DFMU) that computes importance scores for model computational blocks using only a single forward and backward pass. Based on knowledge-preserving pruning techniques to enable faster convergence with minimal data requirements.
AI.result: Achieves 40% higher retain-accuracy using just 13% of data samples compared to state-of-the-art methods across various public datasets. Also averages 88% faster processing time for forgetting a given class.
AI.conclusion: DFMU provides an efficient alternative to retraining-based unlearning, significantly reducing computational requirements while maintaining model performance when removing elements from trained models.

title: The Interplay of Harness Design and Post-Training in LLM Agents
summary: Tool-integrated LLM agents are often wrapped within a harness: the scaffolding that determines which tools are exposed, how they are described, and what auxiliary information accompanies each per-step observation. While agents are routinely post-trained, this scaffolding is typically treated as a fixed engineering detail, with design effort limited to the training-free regime. Moreover, existing post-training algorithms assume a static environment, even though tool environments and tasks often shift upon deployment. To address this gap, we extend $\texttt{ALFWorld}$ (i) to treat the harness as a controllable design dimension and (ii) to support evaluation under task and tool environment shifts. Building on this, we systematically analyze how the harness design influences post-training in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution (OOD) settings. We empirically show that harness-aware post-training not only improves in-distribution performance but also enables agents to robustly adapt to OOD settings. Under a harness with minimal design effort, post-training suffers a drastic performance drop under stronger tool environment shifts, further highlighting the importance of harness-aware post-training under such shifts.

AI.tldr: Tool-integrated LLM agents need harness-aware post-training to adapt to task and tool environment shifts, improving both in-distribution and out-of-distribution performance.
AI.motivation: Current tool-integrated LLM agents treat scaffolding/harness design as fixed engineering detail, with post-training assuming static environments, but real deployment involves shifts in tasks and tool environments.
AI.method: Extended ALFWorld to treat harness as controllable design dimension and support evaluation under task/environment shifts. Systematically analyzed how harness design influences post-training in both in-distribution and OOD settings.
AI.result: Harness-aware post-training improves in-distribution performance and enables robust adaptation to OOD settings. Without harness consideration, post-training suffers severe performance drops under strong tool environment shifts.
AI.conclusion: Harness-aware post-training is crucial for LLM agents to adapt to real-world deployment shifts in tasks and tool environments, and should be systematically considered rather than treated as fixed engineering detail.

title: The Generalization Spectrum: A Chromatographic Approach to Evaluating Learning Algorithms
summary: Traditional evaluations measure a learning algorithm's final performance on an i.i.d. test set, reducing learning to a single aggregate score. This approach obscures a fundamental question: to what extent does learning from a specific example generalize to others? Such per-sample generalization, akin to learning by analogy in human cognition, captures how far the knowledge extracted from one example can transfer, yet remains invisible to standard benchmarks. We introduce the Generalization Spectrum, an evaluation framework designed to expose this hidden dimension. For each training example, we construct a controlled suite of test variants arranged by increasing transfer distance, from exact recall to implementation transfer across languages, context transfer under complete narrative re-framing, category-matched in-domain problems, and an unpaired baseline. By tracking performance across these distances, we reveal not just whether an algorithm learns, but how far that learning extends. We instantiate this framework on competitive programming, using a selection-and-synthesis pipeline seeded with recent problems to mitigate contamination. We first compare three canonical learning paradigms under matched memorization. RL converts memorization into near-transfer more efficiently than SFT-family baselines, while ICL exhibits strong but correspondence-dependent transfer. We then use the Spectrum to diagnose within-family variants. The resulting profiles show that local gains need not expand the generalization radius: abstractions and hints mainly lift local transfer, RFT preserves a stronger far-transfer tail than reference SFT, and self-distillation or hint-assisted RL can reduce far transfer even when local transfer or optimization improves.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Generalization Spectrum, a framework that evaluates how learning from individual training examples transfers across increasing distances, revealing not just whether algorithms learn but how far their learning extends, applied to competitive programming.
AI.motivation: Traditional evaluations with i.i.d. test sets reduce learning to a single aggregate score, obscuring the fundamental question of per-sample generalization - how knowledge from specific examples transfers to others, analogous to human learning by analogy.
AI.method: For each training example, construct a controlled suite of test variants arranged by increasing transfer distance: from exact recall to implementation transfer across languages, context transfer with narrative re-framing, category-matched in-domain problems, and unpaired baseline. Apply this framework to competitive programming using a selection-and-synthesis pipeline seeded with recent problems to mitigate contamination.
AI.result: Comparing three learning paradigms under matched memorization: RL converts memorization into near-transfer more efficiently than SFT-family baselines, while ICL shows strong but correspondence-dependent transfer. Within-family variants: abstractions and hints mainly lift local transfer, RFT preserves stronger far-transfer tail than reference SFT, and self-distillation or hint-assisted RL can reduce far transfer despite local improvements.
AI.conclusion: The Generalization Spectrum framework exposes hidden dimensions of learning transfer, revealing that local gains don't necessarily expand generalization radius, and provides diagnostic insights into how different learning approaches transfer knowledge across distances.

title: Towards Robust EEG Decoding Based on Riemannian Self-Attention
summary: Brain-Computer Interface (BCI) based on electroencephalography (EEG) enables direct interaction between the brain and external environments and has significant applications in assistive technologies, medical rehabilitation, and entertainment. Recently, EEG decoding methods based on Symmetric Positive Definite (SPD) learning have demonstrated superior performance. However, these methods typically employ basic network architectures and do not explicitly capture local relationships between EEG signals. This limitation is problematic for EEG signals due to their inherently low Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR). Moreover, most existing Riemannian manifold-based methods are restricted to specific metrics. The most widely used is the Affine-Invariant Metric (AIM). However, it has a quadratic dependency on the SPD matrices and cannot handle ill-conditioned SPD matrices, which hinders the effectiveness of networks. In contrast, the Bures-Wasserstein Metric (BWM) exhibits linear dependence on SPD matrices and demonstrates superior performance for ill conditioning. To overcome these challenges, we propose a Riemannian self-attention network based on the BWM. Additionally, the recently introduced power-deformed generalized Bures-Wasserstein metric reveals a nonlinear relationship between SPD matrices and matrix power deformation. This metric provides a more nuanced representation of the geometric structure of the SPD manifold. Consequently, we extend our model to a learnable version. For simplicity, we refer to it as GBWAtt. Experimental results on three EEG benchmarking datasets validate the robustness and effectiveness of our proposed method. The code is available at https://github.com/jissc/GBWAtt.

AI.tldr: A Riemannian self-attention network based on the Bures-Wasserstein metric that addresses limitations in EEG signal processing by capturing local relationships and handling ill-conditioned SPD matrices, outperforming existing Riemannian methods on EEG datasets.
AI.motivation: Current EEG decoding methods using SPD learning have limitations: they use basic network architectures that don't capture local EEG signal relationships, which is problematic given EEG's low SNR. Most Riemannian methods are restricted to specific metrics like AIM, which has quadratic dependency on SPD matrices and can't handle ill-conditioned SPD matrices effectively.
AI.method: Proposes GBWAtt, a Riemannian self-attention network based on the Bures-Wasserstein Metric (BWM) which has linear dependency on SPD matrices and handles ill conditioning better than AIM. Extends to a learnable version using the power-deformed generalized Bures-Wasserstein metric to capture nonlinear relationships between SPD matrices through matrix power deformation.
AI.result: Experimental results on three EEG benchmarking datasets validate the robustness and effectiveness of the proposed method. The code is publicly available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: The proposed GBWAtt model successfully addresses limitations of existing Riemannian manifold methods for EEG decoding by using BWM-based self-attention to capture local relationships, handle ill-conditioned SPD matrices, and provide more nuanced geometric representations of SPD manifolds.

title: Distill on a Diet: Efficient Knowledge Distillation via Learnable Data Pruning
summary: Knowledge Distillation (KD) is widely used to obtain compact models for efficient inference in resource-constrained environments. Yet the computational overhead of the distillation process itself is often overlooked, raising the question of whether a better student model can be obtained with less data and less compute via data pruning. However, existing data pruning methods are not designed for KD: some introduce substantial overhead, such as obtaining training dynamics through retraining, while others rely on heuristic selection rules that fail to capture what KD actually requires, often resulting in suboptimal subsets. To address these issues, we propose IF-Beta, an efficient data pruning framework that combines influence functions with a learnable sampling policy. Empirically, we first demonstrate that influence functions can serve as an effective and efficient estimator of sample impact in KD settings, where only a pretrained teacher is available. Building on this, our sampling policy is specifically parameterized by a Beta distribution, whose highly flexible two-parameter family allows the policy to adapt to diverse pruning regimes rather than being tied to fixed heuristic forms. Next, we formulate KD pruning as optimizing this policy through a bilevel objective, where the inner loop operates in the teacher feature space with a KD-aligned objective, enabling fast proxy training, while the outer loop updates the policy parameters to maximize distillation performance. This design ensures that IF-Beta is both computationally efficient and inherently aligned with the goals of KD. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet show that IF-Beta consistently outperforms other baselines across a wide range of pruning ratios. Remarkably, IF-Beta enables students trained on less data and less compute to surpass the performance of students distilled on the full dataset.

AI.tldr: IF-Beta is an efficient data pruning framework for Knowledge Distillation that uses influence functions and a learnable Beta distribution sampling policy to select optimal training subsets, reducing compute while improving student performance.
AI.motivation: Current data pruning methods are not designed for Knowledge Distillation (KD) - they either introduce substantial computational overhead (like retraining for training dynamics) or use heuristic selection rules that fail to capture what KD actually requires, resulting in suboptimal subsets. The computational overhead of the distillation process itself is often overlooked despite KD being used for efficient inference.
AI.method: IF-Beta combines influence functions with a learnable sampling policy. Influence functions serve as an efficient estimator of sample impact in KD settings. The sampling policy is parameterized by a Beta distribution (flexible two-parameter family) that adapts to diverse pruning regimes. The framework uses a bilevel optimization: inner loop operates in teacher feature space with KD-aligned objective for fast proxy training, while outer loop updates policy parameters to maximize distillation performance.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10/100 and ImageNet show IF-Beta consistently outperforms other baselines across a wide range of pruning ratios. Remarkably, students trained with IF-Beta on less data and less compute can surpass the performance of students distilled on the full dataset.
AI.conclusion: IF-Beta provides an efficient, KD-aligned data pruning framework that successfully addresses the limitations of existing methods, enabling better student models with reduced computational requirements through intelligent data selection.

title: Beyond One-Size-Fits-All: Diagnosis-Driven Online Reinforcement Learning with Offline Priors
summary: Online reinforcement learning (RL) agents increasingly depend on knowledge acquired offline to achieve practical efficiency. Originally studied in offline-to-online RL, this paradigm now spans foundation model post-training and embodied intelligence, with prior types expanding from offline datasets and pre-trained policies to increasingly diverse knowledge sources such as multimodal foundation models and generative world models. Offline priors have become central to how deep RL is developed and deployed. However, this reliance introduces a challenge that the prevailing benchmark-driven paradigm cannot resolve: because prior validity varies across deployments and shifts during training, no single approach to managing it is universally optimal, and benchmark rankings offer limited guidance for real-world deployments. Rather than pursuing universal solutions, we argue that the field should shift to diagnosis-driven tension management, in which deployment-specific evidence guides how the learner relates to its priors throughout training, enabling both flexible and adaptive deployment. We support this position with a framework characterizing how priors reshape online optimization through three functional roles, controlled experiments demonstrating help-or-hurt reversals, cross-domain evidence from foundation model post-training to embodied intelligence, and engagement with five substantive counterarguments.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that offline priors in reinforcement learning need diagnosis-driven tension management instead of universal benchmark solutions, as prior validity varies across deployments and changes during training.
AI.motivation: Online RL agents increasingly rely on offline knowledge, but the benchmark-driven paradigm fails to address that prior validity varies across deployments and shifts during training, making no single approach universally optimal.
AI.method: The paper proposes diagnosis-driven tension management where deployment-specific evidence guides how learners relate to their priors throughout training, supported by a framework characterizing how priors reshape online optimization through three functional roles, controlled experiments, cross-domain evidence, and engagement with counterarguments.
AI.result: The authors present evidence showing help-or-hurt reversals of priors, cross-domain consistency from foundation model post-training to embodied intelligence, and effectively engage with five substantive counterarguments to their position.
AI.conclusion: The RL field should shift from pursuing universal solutions to diagnosis-driven tension management, enabling both flexible and adaptive deployment by using deployment-specific evidence to guide how learners relate to their priors throughout training.

title: Leaking Circuit Secrets: Gradient Leakage Attacks on Graph Neural Networks
summary: As graph neural networks (GNNs) become standard tools for critical tasks in circuit design and analysis, their security and privacy risks require careful attention. Here, we present the first comprehensive evaluation of gradient leakage attacks (GLAs) on GNNs in circuit-design and hardware-security tasks, a practical threat that has been largely overlooked. We assess state-of-the-art (SOTA) GNNs, including GraphSAGE, GCN, GIN, and GAT, trained on standard netlist benchmarks (ISCAS'85, EPFL, and TrustHub), for their fundamental vulnerability to GLAs. We find that GLAs can expose sensitive information, such as gate types and distinctive properties of hardware Trojans, which may assist adversaries in analyzing logic locking schemes or evading Trojan detection mechanisms. Our analysis shows that these risks are influenced by architectural features, with attention mechanisms (GAT) exacerbating leakage, while injective aggregation (GIN) provides comparatively stronger resilience. We further evaluate several SOTA defense techniques, including differential privacy, gradient clipping, secure aggregation, model compression with quantization, and adversarial training. We find that these techniques improve resilience only in specific settings and can also compromise model performance. Overall, our work provides key insights toward privacy-preserving GNNs and highlights the need for more robust and efficient defenses. We release our full methodology and artifacts.

AI.tldr: This paper presents the first comprehensive evaluation of gradient leakage attacks on graph neural networks in circuit design and hardware security tasks, revealing significant privacy risks and evaluating various defense techniques.
AI.motivation: As GNNs become standard tools for critical circuit design and analysis tasks, their security and privacy risks have been largely overlooked. There is a need to understand the vulnerability of GNNs to gradient leakage attacks in hardware security contexts.
AI.method: The authors conducted a comprehensive evaluation of gradient leakage attacks on state-of-the-art GNNs (GraphSAGE, GCN, GIN, GAT) trained on standard netlist benchmarks (ISCAS'85, EPFL, TrustHub). They assessed both attack effectiveness and defense techniques including differential privacy, gradient clipping, secure aggregation, model quantization, and adversarial training.
AI.result: GLAs can expose sensitive information like gate types and hardware Trojan properties, potentially aiding adversaries in analyzing logic locking schemes or evading Trojan detection. Attention mechanisms (GAT) exacerbate leakage while injective aggregation (GIN) provides stronger resilience. Defense techniques only improve resilience in specific settings and often compromise model performance.
AI.conclusion: The work provides key insights toward privacy-preserving GNNs and highlights the need for more robust and efficient defenses. The findings show that current GNNs in hardware security are vulnerable to gradient leakage attacks, and existing defenses are insufficient, calling for new security approaches.

title: Learning Subset-Shared Invariances for Domain Generalization with Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Domain generalization (DG) aims to learn a model from one or more source domains that generalizes to an unseen target domain without accessing target data during training. A common approach enforces invariance of representations across all source domains, assuming predictive structure is globally shared. However, we demonstrate that enforcing invariance across more domains gradually restricts the feasible representation space, discarding transferable predictive factors that are not universally shared. To address this limitation, we propose subset-shared invariance, where predictive structure is assumed stable only within domain subsets. We implement this principle with a mixture-of-experts architecture, where each expert aligns the specific domains it serves and a routing mechanism composes subset-invariant components for prediction. This creates a routing-conditioned invariance, jointly learned with the representation. To facilitate effective decomposition, we develop training objectives that encourage selective alignment, confident and balanced routing, and diverse expert specialization. Experiments on DomainBed benchmarks demonstrate improved out-of-domain generalization and greater robustness under increasing domain heterogeneity. Our results suggest that DG should move beyond enforcing a single global invariance and instead model invariance through partially shared structure across domain subsets.

AI.tldr: DG should move beyond enforcing global invariance across all domains and instead model invariance through partially shared structure across domain subsets, implemented via a mixture-of-experts architecture with routing-conditioned invariance.
AI.motivation: Enforcing invariance across all source domains in domain generalization gradually restricts the feasible representation space, discarding transferable predictive factors that are not universally shared across all domains.
AI.method: Propose subset-shared invariance where predictive structure is stable only within domain subsets. Implement with mixture-of-experts architecture where each expert aligns specific domains, with routing mechanism composing subset-invariant components for prediction (routing-conditioned invariance). Develop training objectives for selective alignment, confident/balanced routing, and diverse expert specialization.
AI.result: Experiments on DomainBed benchmarks show improved out-of-domain generalization and greater robustness under increasing domain heterogeneity compared to methods enforcing single global invariance.
AI.conclusion: DG should not enforce a single global invariance but instead model invariance through partially shared structure across domain subsets, as this approach better handles domain heterogeneity and preserves transferable predictive factors not universally shared.

title: Memory-Efficient Policy Libraries with Low-Rank Adaptation in Reinforcement Learning
summary: When fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs), there has been success in minimizing both memory usage and computation with Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT), like Low Rank Adaptation (LoRA). In this article, we have explored whether this approach is transferable to the world of robotics and Reinforcement Learning (RL), allowing learning with reduced memory usage and improved computational performance. Specifically, we focused on a version of multi-task robotics, where a library of specialist policies are created. In such a library memory efficiency is especially important. We used a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm and fine-tuned a baseline model to different tasks using LoRA. Our results demonstrate that, depending on the hyperparameters, LoRA can minimize memory usage by a factor of 20-160 compared to full fine-tuning of all layers. This implies a 90-95% storage saving when deploying a library of many (10-50) specialized policies, which can be the differentiating factor between being able to store the entire library in memory or having to use swap-memory in an applied robotics setting. At the same time, our results indicate that there is no significant difference in the success-rate between full fine-tuning and LoRA fine-tuning for the selected tasks.

AI.tldr: LoRA-based PEFT enables memory-efficient fine-tuning of RL policies for robotics tasks, achieving 20-160x memory reduction with comparable performance to full fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) methods like LoRA, successful in LLMs, can be transferred to robotics and RL settings, particularly for memory-efficient creation of specialist policy libraries in multi-task robotics.
AI.method: Used PPO algorithm and fine-tuned a baseline model to different tasks using LoRA, comparing it against full fine-tuning of all layers in multi-task robotics scenarios where specialist policies are created.
AI.result: LoRA reduced memory usage by 20-160x compared to full fine-tuning, enabling 90-95% storage savings when deploying libraries of 10-50 specialized policies. No significant difference in success-rate between LoRA and full fine-tuning for selected tasks.
AI.conclusion: LoRA-based fine-tuning is effectively transferable to robotics RL, providing substantial memory efficiency without sacrificing performance, making it feasible to store entire policy libraries in memory rather than using swap-memory.

title: Cellular Predictions on the Move: What about Data?
summary: Mobile cellular load forecasting is native to network resource optimization and delivery of services with reliability, latency and quality guarantees. The mainstream of machine learning research in the area is focused primarily on developing powerful learning structures for improved prediction accuracy. The data used for forecasting traditionally belong to the cellular domain and at most contain exogenous information about the surroundings of the base stations. We approach the prediction task from the perspective of data as a vital component of any data learning process. We hypothesize that substantial improvements could be achieved when the data inform on the processes that create the cellular load. Specifically, we propose to characterize the population dynamics -- the potential number of cellular traffic sources and their mobility -- in addition to employing historical time series of mobile data traffic. We validate our hypothesis for the rarely examined highway scenario. Comprehensive experiments show forecasting improvements on the order of $60\%$ due to the use of these data alone.

AI.tldr: Using population dynamics data (potential users and their mobility) alongside cellular traffic data improves highway mobile load forecasting by ~60%.
AI.motivation: Current mobile load forecasting research focuses mainly on improving learning structures/accuracy, but neglects the importance of data quality and relevant contextual information about what creates cellular load.
AI.method: Propose characterizing population dynamics (potential traffic sources and their mobility) in addition to historical mobile data traffic time series, focusing on highway scenarios which are rarely examined.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments show forecasting improvements on the order of 60% due to using population dynamics data alone.
AI.conclusion: Substantial forecasting improvements can be achieved by using data that informs on the processes creating cellular load (population dynamics), not just historical traffic patterns, especially in under-examined scenarios like highways.

title: Black-Box Assisted Regression: Phase Transitions and Minimax Optimality
comment: 23 pages, 3 figures. Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: Foundation models are often used as fixed black-box predictors for downstream tasks with limited labeled data, but their predictions may be biased and unsafe to trust blindly. We study this setting through black-box assisted nonparametric regression: a learner observes labeled samples and can query a fixed predictor $f_0$, while the target $f^*$ is close to $f_0$ in $L_2(P_X)$ up to an unknown radius $δ$. We give a finite-sample minimax characterization showing a phase transition at $δ_c(n) \asymp n^{-β/(2β+d)}$, with leading risk $\min\{δ^2, n^{-2β/(2β+d)}\}$. We then analyze a Safe Residual Estimator: it learns a correction around $f_0$, initializes the residual head at zero so the initial predictor equals $f_0$, and uses holdout selection to revert to $f_0$ when the learned correction is not supported by validation data. Here, "safe" means avoiding negative transfer, i.e., performing worse than the black-box predictor alone. The estimator matches the leading minimax term up to an additive validation-selection cost. Synthetic regression experiments verify the predicted phase transition, while CIFAR-100 with CLIP and AG News with Qwen3-8B provide practice-facing evidence that the same residual-correction tradeoff is useful beyond the formal squared-loss regression setting.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a safe residual estimator for black-box foundation models that prevents negative transfer by learning corrections to the base predictor only when beneficial, with theoretical guarantees matching minimax optimal rates.
AI.motivation: Foundation models are often used as black-box predictors for downstream tasks, but their predictions can be biased and unsafe to trust blindly. The challenge is how to safely leverage these models when limited labeled data is available without risking negative transfer (performing worse than using the black-box predictor alone).
AI.method: The paper introduces the "Safe Residual Estimator" that learns a correction around a fixed black-box predictor f₀. The method initializes the residual head at zero (so initial predictor equals f₀), then uses holdout selection to revert to f₀ when the learned correction isn't supported by validation data. This ensures safety by avoiding negative transfer.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows a phase transition at δ_c(n) ≍ n^{-β/(2β+d)} with leading risk min{δ², n^{-2β/(2β+d)}}. The Safe Residual Estimator matches the leading minimax term up to an additive validation-selection cost. Experiments on synthetic data verify the predicted phase transition, and real-world experiments with CIFAR-100/CLIP and AG News/Qwen3-8B show the residual-correction approach works beyond formal squared-loss settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed safe residual correction approach provides a theoretically grounded method for safely improving upon black-box foundation models while guaranteeing no negative transfer, with practical effectiveness demonstrated in both synthetic and real-world scenarios.

title: Bridging Spherical Black-Box Optimizers
summary: When gradient information is unavailable, black-box optimization (BBO) methods provide a practical alternative. While Evolution Strategies (ES), Consensus-Based Optimization (CBO), Optimization via Integration (OVI), and related methods have each been studied independently, their connections remain underexplored. We unify these approaches within a common theoretical framework, revealing that they differ primarily in two design choices: fitness aggregation (controlling sharpness preference) and consensus scope (controlling modality). Leveraging these insights, we introduce hybrid optimizers that interpolate between existing methods. Our ES-OVI hybrid allows explicit control over the preference for flat minima, enabling a trade-off between performance and robustness in continuous control tasks. Our CBO-OVI hybrids combine the higher-dimensional efficiency of parametric methods with the multimodal capabilities of particle-based approaches, achieving competitive results on language model merging under limited evaluation budgets. We validate our methods on standard BBO benchmarks and higher-dimensional locomotion tasks, demonstrating that the hybrid methods can outperform their constituent algorithms.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a unified theoretical framework connecting Evolution Strategies (ES), Consensus-Based Optimization (CBO), and Optimization via Integration (OVI), revealing they differ in fitness aggregation and consensus scope design choices. Hybrid methods combining ES-OVI and CBO-OVI are introduced, enabling explicit control over flat minima preference and efficient multimodal optimization.
AI.motivation: Gradient information is often unavailable in black-box optimization problems, requiring alternative methods like ES, CBO, and OVI. While these methods have been studied independently, their connections and relationships remain underexplored, limiting understanding of their underlying principles and potential for creating improved hybrid approaches.
AI.method: The authors develop a common theoretical framework that unifies ES, CBO, and OVI approaches. They identify two key design choices that differentiate these methods: 1) fitness aggregation (controlling sharpness preference) and 2) consensus scope (controlling modality). Based on these insights, they create hybrid optimizers: ES-OVI for controlling flat minima preference, and CBO-OVI for combining parametric efficiency with multimodal capabilities.
AI.result: The ES-OVI hybrid enables explicit trade-off between performance and robustness in continuous control tasks by controlling preference for flat minima. CBO-OVI hybrids achieve competitive results on language model merging under limited evaluation budgets by combining higher-dimensional efficiency with multimodal capabilities. Both hybrid methods outperform their constituent algorithms on standard BBO benchmarks and higher-dimensional locomotion tasks.
AI.conclusion: The unified framework provides new insights into black-box optimization methods by revealing their fundamental design choices. The hybrid approaches demonstrate practical benefits, with ES-OVI offering controlled trade-offs between performance and robustness, and CBO-OVI enabling efficient multimodal optimization. This work advances the understanding and practical application of gradient-free optimization methods.

title: Deep Neural Networks with Ordinal Loss for Medical Applications
summary: In many prediction problems in medical applications, target labels exhibit an inherent ordinal structure, where class ordering reflects clinically meaningful severity levels. The cost associated with misclassification is often non-uniform and asymmetric, as errors between distant ordinal categories may have substantially more severe consequences than errors between adjacent ones, and overestimating disease severity may have different clinical implications than underestimating it. Traditional loss functions such as multi-class cross-entropy treat all misclassifications equally and fail to incorporate this ordering information. Recent advances in ordinal regression aim to address this limitation by integrating rank-based structures into deep learning models. In this work, we introduce the \textbf{Ordinal Cross-Entropy (OCE)} framework, a general and architecture-independent approach for learning from ordinal data. The proposed method extends the standard cross-entropy formulation to account for misclassification severity through an ordinal cost matrix while preserving the probabilistic interpretation and optimization benefits of the conventional loss. We provide a theoretical analysis of the OCE gradient behavior and show that it yields smoother optimization dynamics and improved ordinal consistency. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that our method achieves lower prediction error costs and better calibration compared to existing state-of-the-art ordinal approaches, establishing OCE as a simple yet effective solution for ordinal regression in deep neural networks.

AI.tldr: OCE framework extends cross-entropy with ordinal cost matrix to handle non-uniform, asymmetric misclassification costs in medical ordinal prediction problems, achieving better performance than existing ordinal regression methods.
AI.motivation: Medical prediction problems often involve ordinal target labels with inherent severity ordering, where misclassification costs are non-uniform and asymmetric. Traditional cross-entropy loss treats all misclassifications equally, failing to incorporate ordering information and the clinical consequences of different types of errors.
AI.method: Proposes Ordinal Cross-Entropy (OCE) framework - an architecture-independent approach that extends standard cross-entropy to account for misclassification severity through an ordinal cost matrix. Preserves probabilistic interpretation and optimization benefits while incorporating rank-based structures.
AI.result: Experiments on benchmark datasets show OCE achieves lower prediction error costs and better calibration compared to state-of-the-art ordinal approaches. Theoretical analysis reveals smoother optimization dynamics and improved ordinal consistency.
AI.conclusion: OCE establishes a simple yet effective solution for ordinal regression in deep neural networks, successfully addressing the limitations of traditional loss functions for medical applications with ordered severity levels.

title: Re-mixing Embeddings for Patient Augmentation in Data Scarce Multiple Instance Learning
comment: Accepted for publication at the 29th International Conference on Medical Image Computing and Computer Assisted Intervention - MICCAI 2026
summary: Data scarcity is a major bottleneck in medical Multiple Instance Learning (MIL), especially for rare diseases or expensive modalities. We introduce a statistically grounded patient augmentation approach that generates realistic patients directly in embedding space. Using Gaussian Mixture Models as a probabilistic clustering approach on pooled instance embeddings from all patients, our method learns disease-specific "recipes"-statistical distributions of instances across unsupervised clusters. New patients are then generated by sampling embeddings from clusters based on learned recipes. Unlike existing methods that require examples from all categories, our method can generate patients offline by re-mixing pooled embeddings. Generated patients are further selected based on uncertainty quantification to improve MIL performance. We evaluate our method across three clinically relevant scarcity scenarios: (i) cross-dataset transfer, where an entirely missing "healthy" class is generated using statistics from an external cohort; (ii) low-data regimes, where class sizes are extremely limited; and (iii) small-cohort non-image tasks, including single-cell RNA-seq and flow cytometry. Across all experiments, our method improves performance over baseline, often outperforming other bag-mixing strategies. Notably, in the missing-class scenario, a performance comparable to full-dataset training is achieved, demonstrating its potential for rare disease diagnostic and privacy-preserving patient augmentation. The code is available at https://github.com/marrlab/RECIPE

AI.tldr: A statistically grounded patient augmentation method for medical MIL that generates realistic patients in embedding space using Gaussian Mixture Models to learn disease-specific "recipes" from pooled instance embeddings, improving performance in data-scarce scenarios.
AI.motivation: Data scarcity is a major bottleneck in medical Multiple Instance Learning, especially for rare diseases or expensive modalities. Existing methods struggle when training data is limited or when examples from certain categories are missing entirely.
AI.method: Uses Gaussian Mixture Models for probabilistic clustering on pooled instance embeddings from all patients to learn disease-specific "recipes" - statistical distributions of instances across unsupervised clusters. New patients are generated by sampling embeddings from clusters based on learned recipes, with additional uncertainty quantification for selecting generated patients.
AI.result: Method improves performance over baselines across three clinically relevant scarcity scenarios: cross-dataset transfer with missing healthy class, low-data regimes with extremely limited class sizes, and small-cohort non-image tasks. Notably achieves performance comparable to full-dataset training in missing-class scenarios and often outperforms other bag-mixing strategies.
AI.conclusion: The proposed patient augmentation approach demonstrates strong potential for rare disease diagnostics and privacy-preserving patient augmentation by enabling realistic patient generation directly in embedding space, effectively addressing data scarcity issues in medical MIL applications.

title: ROAD-VLA: Robust Online Adaptation via Self-Distillation for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: Effective online adaptation of vision-language-action (VLA) models remains challenging, as sparse rewards provide weak supervision for high-dimensional autoregressive action policies. Although self-distillation can in principle provide denser training signals, we find that text-based privileged teachers conditioned on demonstrations, retrieved experiences, or high-level plans are ineffective for VLA adaptation, exposing a modality gap between symbolic guidance and low-level robot actions. We propose ROAD-VLA, an advantage-guided self-distillation framework that constructs a proximal teacher directly in action space by perturbing action-token logits with calibrated advantage estimates. This converts sparse rewards into dense token-level supervision while keeping the teacher close to the current policy. We further derive a policy-improvement lower bound under calibrated advantages and accurate teacher matching. Across seven robotic manipulation environments with in-distribution and out-of-distribution shifts, ROADVLA outperforms PPO in nearly all settings, demonstrating robust online VLA adaptation.

AI.tldr: ROAD-VLA: A self-distillation framework that uses advantage-guided perturbation of action-token logits to convert sparse rewards into dense supervision for robust online adaptation of vision-language-action models in robotic manipulation.
AI.motivation: Online adaptation of vision-language-action (VLA) models is challenging due to sparse rewards providing weak supervision for high-dimensional autoregressive action policies. Existing text-based privileged teachers conditioned on demonstrations, retrieved experiences, or high-level plans are ineffective, exposing a modality gap between symbolic guidance and low-level robot actions.
AI.method: ROAD-VLA uses advantage-guided self-distillation with proximal teachers in action space. It perturbs action-token logits with calibrated advantage estimates to convert sparse rewards into dense token-level supervision while keeping the teacher close to the current policy. The framework includes a policy-improvement lower bound derivation under calibrated advantages and accurate teacher matching.
AI.result: ROAD-VLA outperforms PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) in nearly all settings across seven robotic manipulation environments with both in-distribution and out-of-distribution shifts, demonstrating robust online VLA adaptation.
AI.conclusion: The proposed advantage-guided self-distillation framework effectively addresses the modality gap in VLA adaptation by constructing proximal teachers directly in action space, enabling conversion of sparse rewards into dense token-level supervision and achieving superior performance compared to standard reinforcement learning methods.

title: An Analysis of Posterior Collapse, Parameterization and Initialization in Variational Deep Gaussian Processes
comment: Submitted to the Journal of Machine Learning Research
summary: DGPs are probabilistic models with remarkable prediction performance that concatenate GPs across several layers. Exact inference in DGPs is intractable, and variational inference is often used to approximate the posterior with a parametric distribution tuned by minimizing the Kullback-Leibler divergence. Moreover, finding a good VI approximation is challenging. In particular, a problem of VI is posterior collapse, where VI converges to a variational posterior that matches the prior. In variational DGPs, this implies explaining the data as noise. This work studies posterior collapse in DGPs and identifies its connection to the DSVI algorithm and the widely used linear prior mean function employed in all but the last layer. We show that the benefit of the linear prior mean does not arise from avoiding the non-injective pathology in very deep DGPs, as previously believed, but from improving the conditioning of the optimization problem at initialization. Thus, we propose an alternative initialization of a zero prior mean DGP that mimics a DGP with a linear prior mean at initialization. This enables successful training of DGPs without imposing optimization-driven constraints on the prior, allowing to choose the prior based on modeling assumptions rather than optimization convenience. Our analysis considers three common parameterizations of DGPs and shows that not all of them benefit from a linear prior mean. We also explain why a whitened parameterization of the \DGP provides more stable convergence, something often assumed from experience, but lacking a rigorous analysis. Furthermore, we show that this stability is also beneficial to avoid the posterior collapse problem. Extensive experiments validate our findings: the proposed initialization prevents posterior collapse, improves stability, and achieves performance comparable to (and sometimes better than) DGPs with a linear prior mean.

AI.tldr: This paper studies posterior collapse in Deep Gaussian Processes (DGPs), identifies its connection to DSVI algorithm and linear prior mean functions, proposes an alternative initialization for zero prior mean DGPs, and explains why whitened parameterization provides more stable convergence.
AI.motivation: Deep Gaussian Processes (DGPs) show remarkable prediction performance but face challenges with variational inference. Posterior collapse occurs where variational inference converges to a variational posterior matching the prior, causing data to be explained as noise. The paper aims to understand why linear prior mean functions work better in DGPs and find alternatives that don't impose optimization-driven constraints on the prior.
AI.method: The paper analyzes posterior collapse in DGPs, identifies its connection to DSVI algorithm and linear prior mean functions. Proposes an alternative initialization for zero prior mean DGPs that mimics a DGP with linear prior mean at initialization. Analyzes three common parameterizations of DGPs and explains why whitened parameterization provides more stable convergence.
AI.result: The proposed initialization prevents posterior collapse, improves stability, and achieves performance comparable to (sometimes better than) DGPs with linear prior mean. Shows that not all DGP parameterizations benefit from linear prior mean, and explains the stability benefits of whitened parameterization which also helps avoid posterior collapse.
AI.conclusion: The linear prior mean's benefit comes from improving optimization conditioning at initialization rather than avoiding non-injective pathologies as previously believed. The proposed alternative initialization enables successful training without optimization-driven prior constraints, allowing priors to be chosen based on modeling assumptions rather than optimization convenience.

title: Variational Autoencoder Layer
summary: Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) belong to a family of autoencoders with probabilistic properties, making them well suited for generating data by producing a smooth and continuous latent space. Despite being introduced over a decade ago, the method continues to be widely adopted in both research and industry for diverse applications. While VAEs are typically used as standalone models, this paper introduces a novel approach to integrate them as a neural network layer. Furthermore, a new training strategy is proposed for models incorporating these layers, and their performance is thoroughly analyzed.

AI.tldr: VAEs are integrated as neural network layers with a novel training strategy, moving beyond their typical standalone use.
AI.motivation: While VAEs have been widely used for over a decade as standalone models for data generation due to their probabilistic properties and smooth latent spaces, there's untapped potential in integrating them directly as neural network layers rather than separate components.
AI.method: The paper introduces a novel approach to integrate VAEs as neural network layers (not just standalone models) and proposes a new training strategy specifically designed for models that incorporate these VAE layers.
AI.result: The performance of models incorporating these VAE layers is thoroughly analyzed, suggesting empirical evaluation of how this integration affects model behavior and capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Integrating VAEs as neural network layers represents a novel architectural advancement that could enhance model capabilities beyond traditional standalone VAE usage, with specialized training strategies needed for optimal performance.

title: $\text{DT}^2$: Decision-Targeted Digital Twins
summary: A digital twin (DT) is a virtual model of a real-world system that can assist decision-making by simulating scenarios induced by different policies. However, typical machine learning-based DTs do not optimise for this use case. We prove that, when model capacity is limited, training DTs to minimise one-step transition errors can produce suboptimal models for ranking sets of policies according to a reward function. We further show that this holds empirically, even with expressive model classes. To address this, we introduce $\text{DT}^2$, a decision-targeted DT training paradigm. Firstly, $\text{DT}^2$ uses fitted Q-evaluation to estimate values of candidate policies from offline data. A DT is then trained to generate rollouts that preserve pairwise policy rankings derived from these proxy ground-truth values with an architecture-agnostic loss function. We empirically demonstrate the efficacy of our method across a range of settings and architectures. $\text{DT}^2$ consistently improves policy ranking and reduces decision regret during policy selection relative to conventional DT training, both for policies used during training and for unseen policies, while maintaining a good level of raw simulation fidelity.

AI.tldr: Digital twin models trained with conventional transition error minimization are suboptimal for policy ranking decisions. DT² introduces a decision-targeted training paradigm that optimizes DTs specifically for ranking policies by reward, improving decision quality while maintaining simulation fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current digital twins (DTs) are trained to minimize transition prediction errors, but this approach is suboptimal when DTs are used for decision-making tasks like ranking policies by their expected rewards. There's a mismatch between training objective (accurate state transitions) and the actual use case (policy evaluation and selection).
AI.method: DT² uses fitted Q-evaluation to estimate policy values from offline data, then trains the DT with an architecture-agnostic loss function that preserves pairwise policy rankings derived from these proxy ground-truth values, rather than just minimizing transition errors.
AI.result: Empirical results show DT² consistently improves policy ranking accuracy and reduces decision regret during policy selection compared to conventional DT training. This holds for both policies used during training and unseen policies, while maintaining good raw simulation fidelity.
AI.conclusion: Digital twins should be trained with decision objectives in mind when they're intended for policy evaluation and selection. DT²'s decision-targeted training paradigm better aligns DT training with actual use cases, improving decision quality without sacrificing simulation accuracy.

title: Improving Neural Network Training by Decoupling the Magnitude and Direction of Weight Vectors
summary: Modern neural network training relies on optimizers such as Adam and Muon which act on each weight matrix as a single object. Yet every weight matrix carries two distinct quantities -- a \emph{magnitude} and a \emph{direction} -- and all optimizers stepping in the matrix as a whole couple their dynamics: the directional change from an update depends on the current magnitude, while the magnitude drifts as a byproduct of learning the direction, so neither is governed directly by the learning rate. Typical training therefore leans on surrounding recipes such as weight decay and warmup to keep learning stable at scale, though these regulate the coupling only indirectly; other recent methods instead constrain the weight to a fixed-norm sphere, but add no learnable magnitude, leaving scale control to normalization layers alone. We propose \emph{Magnitude--Direction (MD) Decoupling}, an optimizer modification that factorizes each weight into a fixed-norm direction on a hypersphere and learnable per-row and per-column magnitude gains, updated at separate learning rates, all while the model still sees a single fused weight tensor. The method is agnostic to the base optimizer and removes the need for weight decay and warmup. Across both Adam and Muon, MD Decoupling improves on well-tuned baselines, transfers the optimal LR across model width without retuning, and continues to help at scale on large Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models. Treating magnitude and direction as separately controlled quantities thus yields more predictable training dynamics and a simple, broadly applicable improvement to modern optimizers.

AI.tldr: MD Decoupling proposes separating weight magnitude and direction updates with different learning rates, eliminating need for weight decay and warmup while improving training stability and transferability across model scales.
AI.motivation: Current optimizers like Adam and Muon treat weight matrices as single objects, coupling magnitude and direction dynamics. This coupling causes instability that requires auxiliary techniques like weight decay and warmup. The magnitude and direction should be separately controllable for better training dynamics.
AI.method: MD Decoupling factorizes each weight into: (1) a fixed-norm direction on a hypersphere, and (2) learnable per-row and per-column magnitude gains. These components are updated at separate learning rates while presenting a single fused weight tensor to the model. The method is agnostic to base optimizers.
AI.result: Improves performance over well-tuned baselines for both Adam and Muon; enables learning rate transfer across model widths without retuning; scales effectively to large Mixture-of-Experts models; eliminates need for weight decay and warmup.
AI.conclusion: Separating magnitude and direction as independently controlled quantities provides more predictable training dynamics and offers a simple, broadly applicable improvement to modern optimizers.

title: Tensorion: A Tensor-Aware Generalization of the Muon Optimizer
summary: Common first-order optimizers, such as Adam, implicitly treat each parameter block as an unstructured vector, which disregards the multilinear weight structure present in many modern machine learning models. Recent work has shown that exploiting matrix structure can improve optimization dynamics. A notable example is Muon, which performs steepest descent under the spectral norm constraint. We take the next step and introduce Tensorion, a tensor-aware optimizer that extends Muon's constrained optimization perspective from matrices to higher-order tensors. Tensorion is built around a linear minimization oracle (LMO) over a tensor norm ball. The norm is carefully chosen to balance two objectives: tightly bounding the tensor spectral norm, while still keeping the LMO tractable.
