This LMO becomes computable because it reduces to operations on adaptively selected unfolding matrices. Notably, when restricted to order-2 tensors (i.e., matrices), Tensorion recovers Muon exactly. Experiments on tensor-based computer vision problems suggest that Tensorion can offer improved convergence behavior and more stable gradient updates compared with Adam-based and existing tensor-aware baselines in the evaluated settings.

AI.tldr: Tensorion is a new tensor-aware optimizer that extends matrix optimization techniques to higher-order tensors by using a linear minimization oracle over a specially chosen tensor norm ball.
AI.motivation: Existing optimizers like Adam treat parameters as unstructured vectors, ignoring the multilinear weight structure in modern ML models. While recent work showed exploiting matrix structure improves optimization, there's a need to extend this to higher-order tensors.
AI.method: Tensorion uses a constrained optimization perspective with a linear minimization oracle over a tensor norm ball. The norm balances bounding tensor spectral norm while keeping LMO tractable. The LMO reduces to operations on adaptively selected unfolding matrices.
AI.result: Experiments on tensor-based computer vision problems show Tensorion offers improved convergence behavior and more stable gradient updates compared to Adam and existing tensor-aware baselines.
AI.conclusion: Tensorion successfully extends tensor-aware optimization from matrices to higher-order tensors, demonstrating practical benefits in convergence and stability for tensor-structured ML problems.

title: The Inference-Compute Frontier and a Latency-Efficient Architecture for Limit Order Book Prediction
summary:
We study whether a scaling-law-style inference-compute frontier appears in limit order book prediction. Using FI-2010 and a suite of models ranging from small decision trees to neural LOB architectures, we find that the realized empirical frontier of predictive loss versus structural forward work is well summarized by a power law. In particular, with MLPLOB held out as an architecture family, a power-law fit to the low- and mid-compute non-MLPLOB frontier extrapolates across multiple orders of magnitude and attains $R^2=0.941$ on the excluded high-compute MLPLOB target frontier.
  A similar exercise in latency space gives substantially weaker results, showing that latency is not merely noisy compute. We use this gap to motivate FastBiNLOB, a dense axis-separable LOB mixer built from hardware-friendly temporal and feature mixing operations. In a five-seed experiment, FastBiNLOB exceeds the published $y_{10}$ and $y_{100}$ macro-F1 targets at notably lower latency than existing published SOTA architectures.

AI.tldr: The paper finds a power-law scaling relationship between predictive accuracy and computational resources for limit order book prediction, similar to scaling laws in large language models. The authors develop FastBiNLOB, a hardware-efficient architecture that achieves state-of-the-art performance at lower latency.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates whether scaling laws similar to those observed in large language models (where performance scales predictably with compute) exist in limit order book (LOB) prediction. The authors want to understand the relationship between prediction accuracy, computational resources, and latency in financial time series forecasting.
AI.method: The authors use the FI-2010 dataset and test a suite of models ranging from small decision trees to neural LOB architectures. They analyze the empirical frontier of predictive loss versus structural forward work (compute) and find it follows a power law. They then design FastBiNLOB, a dense axis-separable LOB mixer built from hardware-friendly temporal and feature mixing operations, optimized for low latency.
AI.result: A power-law relationship is found between predictive loss and compute resources (R²=0.941), showing scaling laws exist in LOB prediction. However, latency shows weaker correlation with performance, indicating latency is not merely noisy compute. FastBiNLOB exceeds published y₁₀ and y₁₀₀ macro-F1 targets at notably lower latency than existing state-of-the-art architectures in five-seed experiments.
AI.conclusion: Scaling laws similar to those in large language models do exist in limit order book prediction, providing a predictable relationship between compute resources and predictive performance. The gap between compute scaling and latency scaling motivates hardware-efficient architectures like FastBiNLOB, which demonstrates that architectural innovations can achieve state-of-the-art performance with lower latency, making real-time LOB prediction more practical.

title: Is Variational Monte Carlo Robust? Sharp Moment Thresholds and Heavy-tailed Stochastic Optimization
summary: Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) is a central algorithm in electronic structure theory and has gained renewed importance through modern neural-network ansätze such as FermiNet. At its core, VMC seeks ground states by minimizing the Rayleigh quotient by stochastic optimization. In this work, we show that the resulting stochastic optimization problem is intrinsically governed by the nodal geometry of the underlying wave function. More precisely, we establish that properties of the nodal set determine the integrability of the local energy and gradient estimators that drive VMC. For broad and practically relevant ansatz classes, including Slater-Jastrow wave functions with variable-exponent Slater-type orbitals, we prove that these estimators are generically heavy-tailed and fail to admit higher moments. At the same time, for general analytic ansätze, we prove weak moment bounds for the relevant estimators and identify precise low-moment regimes, showing how generic and degenerate nodal structures lead to different integrability thresholds. Building on this analysis, we introduce a new robust variant of VMC $\unicode{x2013}$ coined PS-Clip-VMC $\unicode{x2013}$ which is based on clipping both the local energy and the gradient random variable. We prove that PS-Clip-VMC converges both in expectation and with high probability in the weak moment regime of VMC. Preliminary experiments for training FermiNet on Atoms with up to 18 electrons suggest that PS-Clip-VMC is significantly more robust than standard methods.

AI.tldr: PS-Clip-VMC is a new robust Variational Monte Carlo method using clipping to handle heavy-tailed gradient distributions caused by wave function nodal geometry, improving convergence and stability in neural network wave function optimization.
AI.motivation: Variational Monte Carlo (VMC) with modern neural-network wave functions like FermiNet suffers from unstable optimization due to heavy-tailed local energy and gradient distributions, which arise from intrinsic nodal geometry of wave functions.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of nodal geometry's impact on estimator integrability, followed by development of PS-Clip-VMC - a robust VMC variant that clips both local energy and gradient random variables to handle heavy tails.
AI.result: Proved PS-Clip-VMC converges in expectation and with high probability in weak moment regimes, with preliminary experiments showing significantly better robustness than standard methods for training FermiNet on atoms with up to 18 electrons.
AI.conclusion: Nodal geometry fundamentally determines VMC optimization stability, and PS-Clip-VMC provides a practical solution for robust neural-network wave function training by addressing heavy-tailed estimator distributions through clipping.

title: RevengeBench: Reverse Engineering Code-Space Policies from Behavioral Experiments
summary: For most of scientific history, researchers studying behavior could only infer hidden mechanisms from outward actions: an inverse problem that becomes more tractable when observation is augmented by targeted intervention. We pose a computational analogue: given only behavioral traces of an agent in a game environment, can a learner reconstruct the underlying decision program as executable code, and how much does this reconstruction improve with the ability to design controlled experiments? We introduce RevengeBench, a benchmark of 75 LLM generated, Elo-calibrated policies across five game environments, drawn from CodeClash tournament trajectories. The learner observes the hidden target policy play against sampled opponents and designs behavioral probes in the form of custom opponent policies that elicit informative behavior. It then submits an executable hypothesis, which is evaluated using continuous action-distance metrics. We further validate that recovered code carries informative signal in downstream player-versus-player tournaments. Across twelve frontier LLMs, recovery quality varies substantially (34 to 72% of initial distance closed), with reconstructed policies yielding measurable competitive advantage, particularly for weaker models that otherwise struggle to design effective counter-strategies. Our benchmark positions behavioral recovery of programmatic policies as a tractable inverse problem in code-space, opening a path to opponent modeling, policy interpretability, and the broader question of inferring latent mechanisms from observations.

AI.tldr: A benchmark (RevengeBench) tests how well AI models can reconstruct executable decision programs from behavioral observations, showing that controlled experiments improve reconstruction quality and recovered policies provide competitive advantages in tournaments.
AI.motivation: Scientific research often faces the challenge of inferring hidden mechanisms from outward behaviors—an inverse problem. The authors propose a computational analogue: given behavioral traces of an agent in a game environment, can we reconstruct the underlying decision program as executable code, and does controlled experimentation improve this reconstruction?
AI.method: The authors introduce RevengeBench, a benchmark of 75 LLM-generated, Elo-calibrated policies across five game environments from CodeClash tournament trajectories. Learners observe hidden target policies playing against sampled opponents and design behavioral probes in the form of custom opponent policies to elicit informative behavior. They then submit executable hypotheses evaluated using continuous action-distance metrics.
AI.result: Across twelve frontier LLMs, recovery quality varied substantially (34% to 72% of initial distance closed). Reconstructed policies yielded measurable competitive advantage, particularly for weaker models that otherwise struggle to design effective counter-strategies. The recovered code also carried informative signal in downstream player-versus-player tournaments.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark positions behavioral recovery of programmatic policies as a tractable inverse problem in code-space, opening paths to opponent modeling, policy interpretability, and the broader question of inferring latent mechanisms from observations.

title: BluTrain: A C++/CUDA Framework for AI Systems
summary: Progress in deep learning is, at scale, more a matter of systems engineering than of modelling: the behaviour of a model in training (its throughput, its memory footprint, and the numerical fidelity of the result) is determined less by the architecture itself than by how that architecture is expressed on the hardware. To achieve absolute control over this hardware expression while abstracting away systems complexity to make modelling seamless and eliminating the need for repetitive orchestration logic, BluTrain was architected from first principles as a robust, lightweight, and architecture-general training framework in standard C++ and the core CUDA programming model. Every layer is implemented natively: a typed tensor module with reverse-mode autograd, a linear-algebra library, a caching allocator, a multi-mode distributed-execution module, and an MLIR-based deep-learning compiler. In formal evaluations training a 124M-parameter GPT-2 baseline in FP32 on an 8-GPU 6000 Ada system, BluTrain outperforms industry-standard baselines in both throughput (sustaining an average of 407K tokens/s versus PyTorch's 395K tokens/s) and memory efficiency (achieving up to a 22% footprint reduction), while strictly preserving numerical fidelity and converging to a marginally lower final validation loss. With every layer explicitly open to native tuning, the performance ceiling is the framework's own to raise.

AI.tldr: BluTrain is a C++/CUDA training framework that outperforms PyTorch in throughput and memory efficiency while maintaining numerical fidelity, giving developers fine-grained hardware control.
AI.motivation: Current deep learning frameworks introduce systems complexity and repetitive orchestration logic that obscure hardware-level control, limiting performance optimization and efficient training.
AI.method: Built from first principles in C++ and CUDA with native implementations of all core components: typed tensor modules with autograd, linear algebra library, caching allocator, distributed execution, and MLIR-based compiler.
AI.result: Training a 124M-parameter GPT-2 on 8 GPUs, BluTrain achieved 407K tokens/s throughput (vs PyTorch's 395K) with 22% memory reduction, maintained numerical fidelity, and achieved lower validation loss.
AI.conclusion: BluTrain demonstrates that architecture-native frameworks provide superior hardware control, enabling better performance than industry standards while maintaining mathematical correctness and convergence.
